description: A complete Duolingo-style course covering all 16 chapters of Stewart, Clegg, and Watson's Calculus: Early Transcendentals (9th edition, 2020). Every section appears as a lesson with a student-friendly explanation, key formulas, two worked examples, common mistakes, five practice questions, and full worked answers. Original problems written specifically for this course — not reproductions of the textbook's exercises.
source: Stewart, Clegg, Watson — Calculus: Early Transcendentals, 9th ed. (Cengage, 2021)
audience: Year 9–13 / A-Level / first-year undergraduate, with Cambridge Mathematics ambitions in mind

lessons.title: 1.1 Four Ways to Represent a Function
lessons.explanation: A function f is a rule that assigns to each element x in a set D (the domain) exactly one element f(x) in a set E (the range/codomain). We can represent functions in four equivalent ways: (1) verbally — described in words, (2) numerically — by a table of values, (3) visually — by a graph, and (4) algebraically — by an explicit formula. The graph of f consists of all points (x, f(x)). The vertical line test says: a curve in the xy-plane is the graph of a function of x iff no vertical line crosses the curve more than once. Functions can be piecewise (defined by different formulas on different intervals). A function is even if f(-x) = f(x) (symmetric about the y-axis), and odd if f(-x) = -f(x) (symmetric about the origin). A function is increasing on an interval I if f(x1) < f(x2) whenever x1 < x2 in I; decreasing reverses the inequality.
formulas: Function notation: y = f(x), domain D, range = {f(x) : x ∈ D}
Even function: f(-x) = f(x)
Odd function: f(-x) = -f(x)
Vertical line test: each vertical line meets graph at most once
Absolute value as piecewise: |x| = x if x ≥ 0,  -x if x < 0

examples.setup: Find the domain of f(x) = √(x − 2) / (x − 5).
steps: We need the radicand non-negative: x − 2 ≥ 0, so x ≥ 2.
We need the denominator non-zero: x − 5 ≠ 0, so x ≠ 5.

examples.setup: Determine whether g(x) = x³ − x is even, odd, or neither.
examples.result: g is odd (graph symmetric about the origin).

common_mistakes: Confusing domain restrictions: forgetting that even-root radicands must be ≥ 0 AND denominators ≠ 0 simultaneously.
Assuming a function is even or odd without checking — most functions are neither.
Treating f(-x) as -f(x): they're only equal when f is odd.

questions.prompt: Which curve fails the vertical line test?
questions.solution: x = y² is a sideways parabola; a vertical line at x = 1 hits both (1, 1) and (1, -1).
Denominator zero when x² = 9, so exclude x = 3 and x = -3.

questions.prompt: If f(x) = x⁴ − 3x² + 7, then f is:
questions.solution: f(-x) = x⁴ − 3x² + 7 = f(x), so f is even.

questions.prompt: Compute f(-2) − f(2) where f(x) = x³ + 5x.
questions.solution: f is odd (only odd-power terms), so f(-2) = -f(2), giving f(-2) − f(2) = -2f(2)? Actually: f(2)=8+10=18, f(-2)=-18, so -18 − 18 = -36. Wait — recompute: f(-2)−f(2)=-18−18=-36.

questions.prompt: State the range of f(x) = x² − 4 for x ∈ ℝ.
questions.solution: Minimum at x=0 gives f=-4; values increase without bound.

answers: Q1: x = y² (option C). Vertical line x=1 intersects at y=±1.
Q2: Domain = (-∞,-3) ∪ (-3,3) ∪ (3,∞). Excludes zeros of denominator.
Q3: Even. All exponents are even, so f(-x)=f(x).
Q5: Range = [-4, ∞). Vertex of parabola is the minimum.

lessons.title: 1.2 Mathematical Models: A Catalog of Essential Functions
lessons.explanation: A mathematical model is a function that describes a real-world phenomenon. Common families: linear functions f(x) = mx + b (constant rate of change m); polynomials P(x) = aₙxⁿ + ... + a₁x + a₀ (degree n; quadratics, cubics, quartics, etc.); power functions f(x) = xᵃ (includes roots, reciprocals); rational functions P(x)/Q(x); algebraic functions (built from polynomials by +,−,×,÷,roots); trigonometric functions (sin, cos, tan, …); exponential functions f(x) = bˣ; and logarithmic functions f(x) = log_b x. Modeling workflow: (1) identify the type of function suggested by data, (2) fit parameters, (3) check the model, (4) make predictions.
formulas: Linear: y = mx + b; slope m = (y₂−y₁)/(x₂−x₁)
Quadratic: y = ax² + bx + c, vertex at x = -b/(2a)
Power: y = xᵃ — special cases: a = 1/2 gives √x, a = -1 gives 1/x
Polynomial of degree n: at most n−1 turning points, at most n real zeros
Trig (basic period): sin and cos period 2π; tan period π

examples.setup: A spring obeys Hooke's law: stretching by x cm gives a force F (N). At x = 5, F = 12. Find F when x = 8.
steps: Hooke's law: F = kx (linear, no constant term).

examples.setup: Find the quadratic through (0, 1), (1, 0), (2, 3).
steps: Let y = ax² + bx + c. From (0,1): c = 1.

common_mistakes: Fitting a polynomial of too-high degree to data — overfits noise.
Using radians/degrees inconsistently in trig models.
Forgetting domain restrictions when a model only makes physical sense for certain x.

questions.prompt: A line passes through (2, 5) and (6, 13). Find its slope.
Which function is a power function?
Find the equation of the line through (0, 4) with slope -3.
The polynomial P(x) = x³ − x has degree:
What is the period of f(x) = sin(2x)?

answers: Q2: x² (power function with constant exponent).

lessons.title: 1.3 New Functions from Old Functions
lessons.explanation: We build new functions by transforming old ones. Vertical/horizontal shifts: y = f(x) + c shifts up by c; y = f(x − c) shifts right by c. Stretches/reflections: y = c·f(x) stretches vertically by c (reflects in x-axis if c < 0); y = f(cx) compresses horizontally by c (reflects in y-axis if c < 0). Combinations: (f + g)(x) = f(x) + g(x); similarly for −, ×, ÷. The composition (f ∘ g)(x) = f(g(x)); domain is all x in domain of g such that g(x) is in domain of f. Order matters: f ∘ g ≠ g ∘ f in general.
formulas: Vertical shift: y = f(x) + c (c > 0 up, c < 0 down)
Horizontal shift: y = f(x − c) (c > 0 right, c < 0 left)
Vertical stretch: y = c f(x) (|c| > 1 stretch, |c| < 1 compress)
Horizontal stretch: y = f(x/c)  (|c| > 1 stretch, |c| < 1 compress)
Reflections: y = -f(x) (flip over x-axis); y = f(-x) (flip over y-axis)
Composition: (f ∘ g)(x) = f(g(x))

examples.setup: Given f(x) = √x, sketch (in your head) and describe y = √(x − 3) + 2.
steps: Replace x by (x−3): shift right by 3.
examples.result: Square-root curve translated 3 right and 2 up; starts at (3, 2).

examples.setup: If f(x) = x² and g(x) = x − 1, find f ∘ g and g ∘ f.

common_mistakes: Getting the direction of horizontal shifts backward — y = f(x − 3) shifts RIGHT (because input must be 3 larger to give same output).
Confusing f ∘ g with g ∘ f.
Forgetting to restrict the domain of a composition by the inner function's domain.

questions.solution: Inside arg, signs flip: x + 4 = x − (−4), so left by 4.

questions.prompt: If f(x) = 3x − 2 and g(x) = x², compute (f ∘ g)(2).
If f(x) = √x, give the formula for the curve shifted 1 left and 5 up.
questions.solution: Replace x with x+1, then add 5.

questions.prompt: If f(x) = 1/x and g(x) = x + 2, find (g ∘ f)(x).

lessons.title: 1.4 Exponential Functions
lessons.explanation: An exponential function has the form f(x) = bˣ where the base b > 0 and b ≠ 1. The domain is all real numbers ℝ, and the range is (0, ∞). If b > 1 the function is increasing (growth); if 0 < b < 1 it is decreasing (decay). All exponential graphs pass through (0, 1). Laws of exponents: bˣ⁺ʸ = bˣbʸ, bˣ⁻ʸ = bˣ/bʸ, (bˣ)ʸ = bˣʸ, (ab)ˣ = aˣbˣ. The most important base is e ≈ 2.71828, defined so that the slope of y = eˣ at x = 0 equals 1. Exponential functions model unrestricted growth (populations, compound interest, viral spread) and decay (radioactive substances, cooling).
formulas: f(x) = bˣ, b > 0, b ≠ 1
bˣ⁺ʸ = bˣ · bʸ
bˣ⁻ʸ = bˣ / bʸ
(bˣ)ʸ = bˣʸ
(ab)ˣ = aˣ · bˣ
b⁰ = 1; b⁻ˣ = 1/bˣ
Continuous compound interest: A = Pe^(rt)

examples.setup: Simplify (2³)² · 2⁻¹.
A bacterial culture starts with 500 cells and doubles every 3 hours. Find population after 12 hours.
steps: Doubling every 3 hours means in t hours, exponent t/3.

common_mistakes: Treating bˣ + bʸ as if it equalled b^(x+y) — only PRODUCTS combine that way.
Confusing 0ᵇ (=0) with b⁰ (=1).
Letting bases differ when applying laws — 2³·3² ≠ 6⁵.

questions.prompt: Which is greater for x large positive?
questions.solution: Any exponential with base > 1 eventually outgrows any polynomial.

lessons.title: 1.5 Inverse Functions and Logarithms
lessons.explanation: A function f is one-to-one if f(x₁) ≠ f(x₂) whenever x₁ ≠ x₂. Equivalently, every horizontal line meets the graph at most once (Horizontal Line Test). One-to-one functions have inverses: f⁻¹(y) = x ⇔ f(x) = y. The graph of f⁻¹ is the reflection of f across y = x. The logarithm log_b is the inverse of the exponential bˣ: log_b(bˣ) = x and b^(log_b x) = x. Natural log: ln x = log_e x. Log laws: log(xy) = log x + log y; log(x/y) = log x − log y; log(xʳ) = r log x. Change of base: log_b x = ln x / ln b. Inverse trig functions: sin⁻¹ has range [-π/2, π/2]; cos⁻¹ has range [0, π]; tan⁻¹ has range (-π/2, π/2).
formulas: f⁻¹(f(x)) = x for x in domain of f; f(f⁻¹(y)) = y for y in domain of f⁻¹
Graph of f⁻¹ = reflection of f over y = x
log_b x = y  ⇔  bʸ = x
log(xy) = log x + log y;  log(x/y) = log x − log y;  log(xʳ) = r log x
ln(eˣ) = x;  e^(ln x) = x for x > 0
Change of base: log_b x = (ln x)/(ln b)

examples.setup: Find the inverse of f(x) = (2x + 1)/(x − 3).
Solve 5e^(2x) = 30 exactly.

common_mistakes: Treating f⁻¹(x) as 1/f(x). They are not equal.
Forgetting the horizontal line test — only one-to-one functions have inverses.
Misapplying log laws: log(x + y) is NOT log x + log y.

questions.solution: Quotient rule: log 8 − log 2 = log(8/2) = log 4. Both 'log 4' and the quotient form are right.

lessons.title: 2.1 The Tangent and Velocity Problems
lessons.explanation: Two ancient problems gave birth to calculus. The tangent problem: find the slope of the line tangent to a curve y = f(x) at a point P = (a, f(a)). We approximate by taking a nearby point Q = (x, f(x)) and computing the slope of the secant line PQ: m_PQ = [f(x) − f(a)] / (x − a). As Q approaches P (i.e., x → a), the secant slopes approach the tangent slope. The velocity problem: an object's position is s(t). The average velocity from time a to time t is [s(t) − s(a)] / (t − a). The instantaneous velocity at t = a is the limit of average velocities over shorter intervals. Both problems have the same mathematical structure — they're about LIMITS.
formulas: Secant slope: m_PQ = [f(x) − f(a)] / (x − a)
Tangent slope (intuitive): m = lim_{x→a} [f(x) − f(a)] / (x − a)
Average velocity on [a, t]: v_avg = [s(t) − s(a)] / (t − a)
Instantaneous velocity: v(a) = lim_{t→a} [s(t) − s(a)] / (t − a)

examples.setup: Estimate the tangent slope of y = x² at x = 1 using x = 1.1, 1.01, 1.001.
An object falls so that s(t) = 4.9t² (metres, t in s). Estimate v(2) using t = 2.01, 2.001.

common_mistakes: Computing the secant slope at the point itself (gives 0/0, undefined).
Forgetting that average ≠ instantaneous unless the limit is taken.
Sign errors when the interval is to the left of a (denominator negative).

questions.prompt: For f(x) = x², find the secant slope from x = 2 to x = 2.5.
Predict the tangent slope of x² at x = 2 (use limits).
If s(t) = t³, average velocity on [1, 1.1] equals?

questions.solution: By definition, instantaneous velocity = derivative = limit of secant slopes.

lessons.title: 2.2 The Limit of a Function
lessons.explanation: We write lim_{x→a} f(x) = L if we can make the values of f(x) arbitrarily close to L by choosing x sufficiently close to a (but x ≠ a). The value of f at a does NOT matter — the limit only cares about behaviour near a. One-sided limits: lim_{x→a⁻} f(x) = L considers x approaching a from the LEFT only; lim_{x→a⁺} f(x) = L from the RIGHT. The two-sided limit exists iff both one-sided limits exist and are equal. A limit may fail to exist because: (1) one-sided limits disagree (a 'jump'), (2) f grows without bound (we say f → ±∞ — an 'infinite limit', the limit does not exist as a finite number), (3) f oscillates wildly (e.g. sin(1/x) as x → 0).
formulas: lim_{x→a} f(x) = L (informal: f(x) → L as x → a, x ≠ a)
Two-sided exists ⇔ lim_{x→a⁻} f(x) = lim_{x→a⁺} f(x)
Vertical asymptote at x = a: lim_{x→a} f(x) = ±∞
Note: a limit can fail to exist; ∞ is not a real number.

examples.setup: Investigate lim_{x→0} sin(x)/x using a table.
steps: Same from negative side by symmetry (the function is even).

examples.setup: Let f(x) = 1 if x < 2, and f(x) = 3 if x ≥ 2. Find one-sided and two-sided limits at 2.
steps: From the left, f(x) = 1 always: lim_{x→2⁻} f(x) = 1.
From the right, f(x) = 3: lim_{x→2⁺} f(x) = 3.
Since 1 ≠ 3, the two-sided limit does not exist.
examples.result: Left limit 1, right limit 3, two-sided DNE.

common_mistakes: Plugging x = a into f to get the limit — that's only valid for continuous functions.
Saying a limit 'equals infinity' as if ∞ is a number; correctly we say the limit is infinite (does not exist).
Concluding a limit exists from a few sample points — sin(1/x) fools tables.

questions.prompt: If left limit = 5 and right limit = 5, the two-sided limit:
questions.solution: Equality of one-sided limits is exactly the condition for the two-sided limit to equal that common value.

questions.prompt: If lim_{x→1⁻} f(x) = 2 and lim_{x→1⁺} f(x) = 4, does lim_{x→1} f(x) exist? (yes/no)

questions.solution: For x ≠ 2 the expression equals 1; the limit equals 1.

lessons.title: 2.3 Calculating Limits Using the Limit Laws
lessons.explanation: Limit laws (assuming both lim_{x→a} f(x) and lim_{x→a} g(x) exist): the limit of a sum, difference, product, and constant multiple is the sum, difference, product, constant multiple of the limits. The limit of a quotient is the quotient, provided the bottom limit is non-zero. Power and root laws: lim [f(x)]ⁿ = [lim f(x)]ⁿ; lim ⁿ√f(x) = ⁿ√(lim f(x)) (if defined). Direct substitution property: if f is a polynomial or rational function and a is in the domain, then lim_{x→a} f(x) = f(a). For 0/0 'indeterminate forms' (the most common case), try to simplify algebraically — factor, rationalize, or use a known limit. The Squeeze Theorem: if g(x) ≤ f(x) ≤ h(x) near a (except possibly at a) and g, h have the same limit L at a, then f also has limit L.
formulas: Sum: lim (f + g) = lim f + lim g
Product: lim (fg) = (lim f)(lim g)
Quotient: lim (f/g) = (lim f)/(lim g) if lim g ≠ 0
Power/root: lim fⁿ = (lim f)ⁿ; lim √f = √(lim f) if defined
Direct substitution: poly/rational at point in domain ⇒ lim = f(a)
Squeeze Theorem: g ≤ f ≤ h near a, lim g = lim h = L ⇒ lim f = L
Known: lim_{x→0} sin(x)/x = 1; lim_{x→0} (1−cos x)/x = 0

examples.setup: Evaluate lim_{x→1} (x² − 1)/(x − 1).
Evaluate lim_{x→0} (√(x + 4) − 2)/x.
steps: Rationalize: multiply top and bottom by (√(x+4) + 2).

common_mistakes: Applying quotient law when bottom limit = 0 (need to simplify first).
Cancelling factors that aren't actually factors of the original expression.
Using lim sin(x)/x = 1 when x → a ≠ 0 (only valid for x → 0).

lessons.title: 2.4 The Precise Definition of a Limit
lessons.explanation: The ε–δ (epsilon–delta) definition: lim_{x→a} f(x) = L means that for every ε > 0 there exists a δ > 0 such that 0 < |x − a| < δ ⇒ |f(x) − L| < ε. Read it as a challenge–response game: the challenger picks any ε (how close to L we must get); we must produce a δ (how close to a we must be) that guarantees it. Strategy for proving lim_{x→a} f(x) = L: (1) Manipulate |f(x) − L| algebraically, factoring out |x − a|. (2) Bound the remaining factor on a small fixed interval. (3) Set δ = min(that interval radius, ε divided by the bound). For one-sided and infinite limits there are analogous definitions.
formulas: ε–δ definition: ∀ε > 0  ∃δ > 0  s.t. 0 < |x − a| < δ ⇒ |f(x) − L| < ε
Right limit: 0 < x − a < δ ⇒ |f(x) − L| < ε
Left limit:  0 < a − x < δ ⇒ |f(x) − L| < ε
Infinite limit: ∀M > 0 ∃δ > 0 s.t. 0 < |x − a| < δ ⇒ f(x) > M

examples.setup: Prove lim_{x→3} (5x − 2) = 13.
Prove lim_{x→2} x² = 4.
steps: So |x² − 4| < 5|x − 2|. We want < ε, so need |x − 2| < ε/5.

common_mistakes: Picking δ depending on x (it must depend only on ε and a).
Forgetting the 0 < |x − a| condition — limits ignore the value at a.
Choosing δ too large to bound the 'extra factor' (e.g. |x + 2| in the example).

questions.prompt: In the ε–δ definition, who picks ε first?
questions.solution: ε is given; δ is found in response.

questions.prompt: For lim_{x→1} (3x + 4) = 7, find δ corresponding to ε = 0.06.
For lim_{x→0} (2x − 5) = -5, find δ for ε = 0.1.
To prove lim_{x→a} f(x) = ∞ requires showing:
options: For every M, there exists δ with f(x) > M whenever 0 < |x−a| < δ
questions.solution: This is the precise definition for an infinite limit.

answers: Q4: For every M, there exists δ ...

lessons.title: 2.5 Continuity
lessons.explanation: f is continuous at a if lim_{x→a} f(x) = f(a). This compresses three requirements: (1) f(a) is defined, (2) the limit exists, (3) they agree. Discontinuities come in types: removable (limit exists but ≠ f(a) or undefined), jump (one-sided limits differ), infinite (vertical asymptote). f is continuous on an interval if it is continuous at every point. Polynomials, rational functions (on their domains), root functions, exponentials, logarithms, and trig functions are all continuous on their domains. Sums, differences, products, quotients (where the denominator ≠ 0), and compositions of continuous functions are continuous. The Intermediate Value Theorem (IVT): if f is continuous on [a, b] and N is between f(a) and f(b), then there is some c in (a, b) with f(c) = N. Use IVT to prove roots/solutions exist.
formulas: Continuity at a: lim_{x→a} f(x) = f(a)
Right-continuous at a: lim_{x→a⁺} f(x) = f(a)
Continuous on (a, b): continuous at every point
IVT: f continuous on [a, b], N between f(a) and f(b) ⇒ ∃ c ∈ (a, b) with f(c) = N

examples.setup: Where is f(x) = (x² − 1)/(x − 1) discontinuous, and is it removable?
steps: For x ≠ 1, f(x) = x + 1, so lim_{x→1} f = 2.
Defining f(1) = 2 makes it continuous — REMOVABLE discontinuity.

examples.setup: Show x³ + x − 1 = 0 has a root in (0, 1) using IVT.
steps: Let f(x) = x³ + x − 1. f is a polynomial, so continuous everywhere.
Since 0 lies between f(0) and f(1), IVT gives c ∈ (0, 1) with f(c) = 0.

common_mistakes: Calling a discontinuity 'removable' when the two-sided limit doesn't exist.
Applying IVT on an interval where f is not continuous (e.g. across a vertical asymptote).
Confusing 'continuous on an open interval' with 'continuous on a closed interval' (endpoints need one-sided continuity).

questions.prompt: f(x) = 1/x at x = 0 has what kind of discontinuity?
Define f(2) so that f(x) = (x²−4)/(x−2) is continuous at 2.
Which guarantees x⁵ + 2x − 4 = 0 has a real root?
questions.solution: f(1)=-1, f(2)=32 — IVT gives a root.

questions.prompt: If f is continuous on [0, 4] with f(0) = -2 and f(4) = 6, IVT guarantees that some c in (0, 4) satisfies f(c) = ?
options: f cannot be guaranteed any value

lessons.title: 2.6 Limits at Infinity; Horizontal Asymptotes
lessons.explanation: lim_{x→∞} f(x) = L means f(x) gets arbitrarily close to L as x grows without bound; similarly for x → −∞. The line y = L is a HORIZONTAL ASYMPTOTE. Key facts: lim_{x→∞} 1/xʳ = 0 for any r > 0. For rational P(x)/Q(x) as x → ±∞, divide numerator and denominator by the highest power of x in the denominator. Three cases by degree comparison: (i) deg P < deg Q ⇒ limit = 0; (ii) deg P = deg Q ⇒ limit = ratio of leading coefficients; (iii) deg P > deg Q ⇒ limit is ±∞ (no horizontal asymptote in that direction). Exponential growth dominates polynomial growth: lim_{x→∞} eˣ/xⁿ = ∞ for every n. For trig functions: lim_{x→∞} sin x and lim_{x→∞} cos x do not exist (oscillate).
formulas: lim_{x→∞} 1/xʳ = 0 for any r > 0
lim_{x→∞} eˣ = ∞;  lim_{x→-∞} eˣ = 0
Horizontal asymptote y = L: lim_{x→±∞} f(x) = L
Rational function HA: ratio of leading coeffs if same degree; y=0 if num lower; none if num higher

examples.setup: Find lim_{x→∞} (3x² + 4)/(2x² − x + 5).
steps: Divide top and bottom by x² (highest power in denom).

examples.setup: Find lim_{x→∞} (√(x² + 1) − x).
steps: Multiply by conjugate: (√(x²+1) − x)·(√(x²+1) + x)/(√(x²+1) + x).

common_mistakes: Treating ∞ like a number — ∞ − ∞ and ∞/∞ are NOT determined.
For x → −∞, forgetting that √(x²) = |x| = −x.
Saying 'no limit' when f → ∞ means 'limit doesn't exist (as a finite number)'.

questions.solution: Same degree, ratio of leading coeffs.

lessons.title: 2.7 Derivatives and Rates of Change
lessons.explanation: The derivative of a function f at a number a, denoted f'(a), is f'(a) = lim_{h→0} [f(a + h) − f(a)] / h, provided the limit exists. Equivalently, f'(a) = lim_{x→a} [f(x) − f(a)] / (x − a). The derivative gives: (1) the slope of the tangent line to y = f(x) at (a, f(a)) — equation y − f(a) = f'(a)(x − a); (2) the instantaneous rate of change of f with respect to x at a; (3) for position s(t), the instantaneous velocity. Other interpretations: marginal cost in economics, current in electricity, reaction rate in chemistry. f'(a) does not always exist — f must be 'smooth' at a (no corners, vertical tangents, or discontinuities).
formulas: Derivative at a: f'(a) = lim_{h→0} [f(a+h) − f(a)]/h
Equivalent form: f'(a) = lim_{x→a} [f(x) − f(a)]/(x − a)
Tangent line: y − f(a) = f'(a)(x − a)
Instantaneous rate of change of y w.r.t. x at a: dy/dx |_{x=a} = f'(a)

examples.setup: Find f'(3) for f(x) = x² + 2 from the definition.
steps: Numerator: f(3+h) − f(3) = 6h + h² = h(6 + h).

examples.setup: Find the equation of the tangent line to f(x) = 1/x at x = 2.

common_mistakes: Plugging h = 0 too early: gives 0/0.
Confusing average rate (secant) with instantaneous rate (tangent).
Sign/algebra errors when expanding (a + h)² or rationalizing.

questions.prompt: If f(x) = x², find f'(5) from definition.
Tangent line slope of f(x) = √x at x = 4.
If s(t) = position, then s'(t) is:
If f(x) = 3x² + 2, find f'(1) from definition.

lessons.title: 2.8 The Derivative as a Function
lessons.explanation: Letting a vary over a function's domain, we get a new function f', the DERIVATIVE FUNCTION: f'(x) = lim_{h→0} [f(x + h) − f(x)] / h. Notations for the derivative: f'(x), y', dy/dx, df/dx, Df, D_x f. f is differentiable at a if f'(a) exists. f is differentiable on an open interval if it is differentiable at every point. Differentiability ⇒ continuity (the converse is FALSE: |x| is continuous but not differentiable at 0 — corner). Three ways differentiability can fail: corner (different one-sided slopes), vertical tangent (slope → ∞), discontinuity. Higher derivatives: f'' = (f')', f''' = (f'')', f^(n) is the nth derivative. Physical: if s(t) is position, s'(t) is velocity, s''(t) is acceleration, s'''(t) is jerk.
formulas: f'(x) = lim_{h→0} [f(x + h) − f(x)] / h
Notations: f'(x) = y' = dy/dx = df/dx = (d/dx) f(x)
Differentiable ⇒ continuous (converse FAILS at corners, vertical tangents)
Higher derivatives: f''(x) = d²y/dx², f'''(x) = d³y/dx³, ..., f^(n)(x) = dⁿy/dxⁿ

examples.setup: Find f'(x) for f(x) = x³ from the definition.
For s(t) = t² − 4t (position), find velocity v(t) and acceleration a(t).

common_mistakes: Forgetting that 'differentiable' is stronger than 'continuous'.
Treating |x| as differentiable at 0 — it has a corner.
Mixing up f'' (second derivative) with [f(x)]² (squared function).

questions.prompt: If f(x) = 5x², find f'(x) from definition.
If s(t) = t³ − t, find acceleration s''(t).

lessons.title: 3.1 Derivatives of Polynomials and Exponential Functions
lessons.explanation: Basic rules for differentiation. Constant rule: d/dx (c) = 0. Power rule: d/dx (xⁿ) = nxⁿ⁻¹ for any real number n. Constant multiple rule: d/dx [cf(x)] = c f'(x). Sum/difference rule: d/dx [f(x) ± g(x)] = f'(x) ± g'(x). For exponentials: the natural exponential is special — d/dx (eˣ) = eˣ. More generally d/dx (bˣ) = bˣ ln b. The number e is the unique base for which d/dx (bˣ) at x = 0 equals 1. These rules let us differentiate any polynomial in one step instead of using the limit definition.
formulas: d/dx (c) = 0
d/dx (xⁿ) = n xⁿ⁻¹ (any real n)
d/dx [c f(x)] = c f'(x)
d/dx [f(x) ± g(x)] = f'(x) ± g'(x)
d/dx (eˣ) = eˣ
d/dx (bˣ) = bˣ ln b

examples.setup: Differentiate f(x) = 4x³ − 5x² + 7x − 2.
steps: Apply power rule term by term.

examples.setup: Differentiate g(x) = √x + 3eˣ.

common_mistakes: Forgetting to subtract 1 from the exponent in the power rule.
Treating d/dx (eˣ) as x e^(x-1) — that's the polynomial rule misapplied.
Differentiating a constant to anything other than 0.

lessons.title: 3.2 The Product and Quotient Rules
lessons.explanation: Product rule: (fg)' = f'g + fg'. Memorise: 'first times derivative of second, plus second times derivative of first'. Quotient rule: (f/g)' = (f'g − fg') / g². Memorise: 'low d-high minus high d-low, all over the square of what's below'. The product rule is NOT (fg)' = f'g'. The quotient rule has a SIGN — easy to flip. Sometimes simplifying first beats the quotient rule; e.g. (x² + x)/x = x + 1, no quotient rule needed.
formulas: Product: (fg)' = f' g + f g'
Quotient: (f/g)' = (f' g − f g') / g²,  g ≠ 0
Reciprocal: (1/g)' = -g'/g²

examples.setup: Differentiate f(x) = x² eˣ.
steps: Product rule with u = x², v = eˣ.

examples.setup: Differentiate g(x) = (x² + 1)/(x − 1).
steps: Quotient rule with u = x² + 1, v = x − 1.

common_mistakes: Writing (fg)' = f'g'.
Reversing the sign in the quotient rule (using f g' − f' g).
Forgetting the square on the denominator.

questions.solution: Standard mnemonic: 'low d-high minus high d-low'.

answers: Q1: sin x + x cos x.

lessons.title: 3.3 Derivatives of Trigonometric Functions
lessons.explanation: All six trig derivatives, in radians: d/dx(sin x) = cos x; d/dx(cos x) = -sin x; d/dx(tan x) = sec² x; d/dx(cot x) = -csc² x; d/dx(sec x) = sec x tan x; d/dx(csc x) = -csc x cot x. The negatives appear in 'co-' functions. Two key limits underpin the derivations: lim_{θ→0} sin θ / θ = 1 and lim_{θ→0} (cos θ − 1)/θ = 0. These derivatives all assume RADIAN measure — degree mode introduces a factor of π/180.
formulas: d/dx (sin x) = cos x
d/dx (cos x) = -sin x
d/dx (tan x) = sec² x
d/dx (cot x) = -csc² x
d/dx (sec x) = sec x tan x
d/dx (csc x) = -csc x cot x
Key limits: lim_{θ→0} (sin θ)/θ = 1; lim_{θ→0} (cos θ − 1)/θ = 0

examples.setup: Differentiate f(x) = x sin x.
steps: Product rule with u = x, v = sin x.
f'(x) = sin x + x cos x.
examples.result: f'(x) = sin x + x cos x

examples.setup: Differentiate g(x) = sec x / (1 + tan x).
steps: Quotient rule. u = sec x, v = 1 + tan x.
u' = sec x tan x; v' = sec² x.
g'(x) = [sec x tan x · (1 + tan x) − sec x · sec² x]/(1 + tan x)².
Factor sec x: = sec x [tan x(1 + tan x) − sec² x]/(1 + tan x)².
Use 1 + tan² x = sec² x: numerator inside = tan x + tan² x − sec² x = tan x − 1.
examples.result: g'(x) = sec x (tan x − 1)/(1 + tan x)²

common_mistakes: Using degrees rather than radians — derivatives change.
Forgetting the negative sign on cos and on cot/csc derivatives.
Confusing d/dx (sin² x) with sin² of derivative — must use chain rule (next section).

questions.solution: Quotient rule on sin/cos gives sec².

answers: Q4: cos x − x sin x.

lessons.title: 3.4 The Chain Rule
lessons.explanation: The chain rule differentiates composite functions. If y = f(g(x)) is differentiable, then dy/dx = f'(g(x)) · g'(x). In Leibniz notation: dy/dx = (dy/du)(du/dx) where u = g(x). Read it as 'derivative of outside (with inside left alone), times derivative of inside.' Combined with earlier rules, the chain rule extends them: d/dx (eᵍ⁽ˣ⁾) = eᵍ⁽ˣ⁾ g'(x); d/dx (sin g(x)) = cos g(x) · g'(x); d/dx [g(x)]ⁿ = n[g(x)]ⁿ⁻¹ g'(x) (the General Power Rule). Apply repeatedly for nested compositions: y = f(g(h(x))) ⇒ y' = f'(g(h(x)))·g'(h(x))·h'(x).
formulas: Chain rule: (f ∘ g)'(x) = f'(g(x)) · g'(x)
Leibniz: dy/dx = (dy/du)·(du/dx)
General Power Rule: d/dx [g(x)]ⁿ = n[g(x)]ⁿ⁻¹ g'(x)
d/dx (e^u) = e^u · du/dx
d/dx (sin u) = cos u · du/dx;  d/dx (cos u) = -sin u · du/dx

examples.setup: Differentiate y = (x³ + 1)⁵.
steps: Outer: u⁵ → 5u⁴; inner u = x³ + 1 → u' = 3x².

examples.setup: Differentiate y = sin(x²).
steps: Outer: sin u → cos u; inner u = x² → u' = 2x.

common_mistakes: Forgetting the inner derivative — most common chain-rule error.
Confusing sin(x²) with sin²(x) (= (sin x)²).
Doubling the chain rule by mistake when the rule has already been applied.

lessons.title: 3.5 Implicit Differentiation
lessons.explanation: Some equations define y as a function of x only IMPLICITLY (e.g. x² + y² = 25, the unit circle). Rather than solve for y first, differentiate both sides w.r.t. x, treating y as a function of x and applying the chain rule whenever you differentiate a y term: d/dx (y) = dy/dx, d/dx (y²) = 2y·dy/dx, d/dx (sin y) = cos y · dy/dx. Then solve the resulting equation for dy/dx algebraically. Often dy/dx will involve both x and y.
formulas: If F(x, y) = 0 defines y implicitly: differentiate, isolate dy/dx.
d/dx (yⁿ) = n yⁿ⁻¹ · dy/dx
d/dx (x · y) = y + x · dy/dx
Slope of tangent on implicit curve at (x₀, y₀): substitute into dy/dx.

examples.setup: Find dy/dx if x² + y² = 25, then the tangent at (3, 4).
Find dy/dx if x³ + y³ = 6xy.

common_mistakes: Forgetting to multiply by dy/dx after differentiating a y term.
Treating x and y as independent — they're related by the equation.
Algebra error when solving for dy/dx (collect y' terms first).

questions.prompt: Differentiating sin y w.r.t. x gives:
If x² + xy + y² = 7, find dy/dx.

lessons.title: 3.6 Derivatives of Logarithmic and Inverse Trigonometric Functions
lessons.explanation: d/dx (ln x) = 1/x for x > 0. With chain rule: d/dx (ln u) = u'/u. For other bases: d/dx (log_b x) = 1/(x ln b). For inverse trig functions: d/dx (sin⁻¹ x) = 1/√(1 − x²); d/dx (cos⁻¹ x) = -1/√(1 − x²); d/dx (tan⁻¹ x) = 1/(1 + x²). The negative versions are for the 'co-' inverses. Logarithmic differentiation: take ln of both sides of y = f(x), differentiate (often easier when f involves products, quotients, or variable exponents like y = xˣ), then multiply by y.
formulas: d/dx (ln x) = 1/x  (x > 0)
d/dx (ln |x|) = 1/x for x ≠ 0
d/dx (log_b x) = 1/(x ln b)
d/dx (sin⁻¹ x) = 1/√(1 − x²)
d/dx (cos⁻¹ x) = -1/√(1 − x²)
d/dx (tan⁻¹ x) = 1/(1 + x²)
Logarithmic differentiation: y = f(x) ⇒ ln y = ln f(x) ⇒ y'/y = (ln f)' ⇒ y' = y · (ln f)'

examples.setup: Differentiate y = ln(x² + 1).
steps: Use d/dx (ln u) = u'/u with u = x² + 1.

examples.setup: Differentiate y = xˣ using logarithmic differentiation.
steps: Differentiate: y'/y = ln x + x·(1/x) = ln x + 1.

common_mistakes: Treating d/dx (ln x) = ln(x)/x.
Mixing up sin⁻¹ derivative (1/√(1−x²)) with cos⁻¹ (-1/√(1−x²)).
Forgetting to multiply back by y at the end of log differentiation.

lessons.title: 3.7 Rates of Change in the Natural and Social Sciences
lessons.explanation: Derivatives express instantaneous rates of change in many fields. PHYSICS: position s(t), velocity v = s', acceleration a = v' = s''. Force F = ma. Density (linear): ρ = dm/dx. Current: I = dQ/dt. CHEMISTRY: reaction rate = d[product]/dt. BIOLOGY: population growth rate dN/dt; instantaneous birth/death rates. ECONOMICS: marginal cost C'(x) = approximate additional cost of producing one more unit; similarly marginal revenue, marginal profit. The COMMON THREAD: every derivative measures the same conceptual thing — instantaneous change of output per unit input.
formulas: Velocity: v(t) = s'(t)
Acceleration: a(t) = v'(t) = s''(t)
Linear density: ρ(x) = m'(x)
Current: I(t) = Q'(t)
Marginal cost: C'(x) ≈ C(x+1) − C(x)

examples.setup: A particle moves so s(t) = t³ − 6t² + 9t (m). Find when it is at rest.
examples.result: Particle at rest at t = 1 s and t = 3 s.

examples.setup: C(x) = 2000 + 3x + 0.01x² is total cost. Find marginal cost at x = 100.
examples.result: Marginal cost ≈ £5 per additional unit at production level 100.

common_mistakes: Confusing v(t) (signed) with speed |v(t)|.
Setting position = 0 instead of velocity = 0 to find rest.
Treating C'(x) as the EXACT cost of unit (x+1) — it's an approximation.

questions.prompt: Q(t) = t² + t coulombs. Find current at t = 3.
If s(t) = sin t, find acceleration at t = π/2.

lessons.title: 3.8 Exponential Growth and Decay
lessons.explanation: Many quantities change at a rate proportional to their current size: dy/dt = ky. The general solution is y(t) = y₀ e^(kt) where y₀ = y(0). If k > 0, it's exponential GROWTH (populations under unrestricted conditions, compound interest). If k < 0, it's exponential DECAY (radioactive substances, drug elimination, cooling per Newton's Law: dT/dt = k(T − T_env)). Half-life: time for half to remain. T_½ = (ln 2)/|k|.
formulas: Differential eq: dy/dt = ky ⇒ y = y₀ e^(kt)
Half-life: T_½ = (ln 2)/|k|
Newton's Law of Cooling: dT/dt = k(T − T_env), T(t) = T_env + (T₀ − T_env) e^(kt)
Continuous compounding: A = P e^(rt)

examples.setup: Bacteria double every 3 hours. Starting with 100, find population after 9 hours.
Carbon-14 has half-life 5730 years. A sample has 60% of original C-14. How old?

common_mistakes: Sign of k for decay — k must be negative.
Mixing half-life with doubling time (formulas same shape but different sign).
Using simple-interest formulas where continuous compounding applies (or vice versa).

questions.prompt: If y = 50 e^(0.1t), what is y(0)?
If half-life is 10 years, find k. (negative number, exact form).
Investment of $1000 at 5% continuous interest. Value after 10 years (exact).
Bacteria triple every hour. Starting with 5, find population at t = 2.
Newton's Law of Cooling says temperature differences decay:

lessons.title: 3.9 Related Rates
lessons.explanation: If two or more quantities are related by an equation and they all change with time, then their RATES are related too. Strategy: (1) Draw a diagram and label variables. (2) Write an equation relating the variables. (3) Differentiate both sides w.r.t. t (every variable carries its own rate via chain rule). (4) Plug in the instantaneous values and known rates to solve for the unknown rate. Note: only substitute the specific numerical values AFTER differentiating, not before.
formulas: Chain rule across t: if A = f(B), then dA/dt = f'(B) · dB/dt
Common geometric relationships:
Right triangle: a² + b² = c²
Circle: A = πr²;  Sphere: V = (4/3)πr³, A = 4πr²
Cone: V = (1/3)πr²h
Cylinder: V = πr²h
Similar triangles: ratios constant

examples.setup: A 5 m ladder leans against a wall. Bottom slides away from wall at 1 m/s. How fast is the top sliding down when bottom is 3 m from wall?
steps: Let x = distance of bottom from wall, y = height on wall. x² + y² = 25.

examples.setup: Spherical balloon's volume increases at 100 cm³/s. Find rate of radius change when r = 5 cm.

common_mistakes: Substituting numerical values BEFORE differentiating — kills any time-dependence.
Failing to apply chain rule on every time-varying variable.
Not drawing a diagram — leads to using wrong relations.

questions.prompt: Square's area increases at 8 cm²/s. Side length when A = 16 is 4. Find ds/dt.
Circle's radius grows at 2 cm/s. Find dA/dt when r = 3 (area).
Cube's edge grows at 0.5 cm/s. Find dV/dt when edge = 10.
In related rates, you should differentiate w.r.t. t:
questions.solution: Substituting values first eliminates time dependence.

questions.prompt: Sphere volume grows at 10 cm³/s. Find dr/dt when r = 5.

lessons.title: 3.10 Linear Approximations and Differentials
lessons.explanation: Near x = a, a differentiable function is approximated by its tangent line: f(x) ≈ L(x) = f(a) + f'(a)(x − a). This is the LINEAR APPROXIMATION (or LINEARISATION). Differentials: if y = f(x), define dy = f'(x) dx. So small changes obey Δy ≈ dy = f'(x) Δx. Useful for: estimating roots and trig values without a calculator, propagating errors in measurement (a measurement error of dx in x produces approximate error f'(x) dx in y), and as the foundation of Newton's method (next sections).
formulas: Linear approximation at a: L(x) = f(a) + f'(a)(x − a)
f(x) ≈ L(x) for x near a
Differential: dy = f'(x) dx
Δy ≈ dy when Δx is small
Relative error ≈ dy/y

examples.setup: Use linearisation of f(x) = √x at a = 9 to estimate √9.04.
The radius of a sphere is measured to be 21 cm with possible error 0.05 cm. Estimate max error in computed volume.

common_mistakes: Using linearisation far from the centre point a (accuracy degrades).
Confusing dy with Δy — dy is a linear estimate of Δy.
Forgetting to evaluate f'(a) before assembling L(x).

questions.prompt: Linearise f(x) = √x at a = 100. Use to estimate √101. (3 d.p.)
Linearise f(x) = sin x at a = 0. Use to estimate sin(0.1).
If f(x) = x² and dx = 0.1 at x = 5, estimate dy.
Use linearisation of f(x) = (1+x)¹⁰ at 0 to estimate f(0.01).

lessons.title: 3.11 Hyperbolic Functions
lessons.explanation: Hyperbolic functions are defined from eˣ and e⁻ˣ: sinh x = (eˣ − e⁻ˣ)/2, cosh x = (eˣ + e⁻ˣ)/2, tanh x = sinh x / cosh x. Identities (parallel to trig but signs different): cosh²x − sinh²x = 1; 1 − tanh²x = sech²x. They appear in cable/chain shapes (catenary y = a cosh(x/a)). Derivatives: d/dx (sinh x) = cosh x; d/dx (cosh x) = sinh x (no minus!); d/dx (tanh x) = sech² x. Inverse hyperbolics like sinh⁻¹ x = ln(x + √(x²+1)) have algebraic-log forms.
formulas: sinh x = (eˣ − e⁻ˣ)/2;  cosh x = (eˣ + e⁻ˣ)/2
tanh x = sinh x / cosh x
cosh² x − sinh² x = 1
d/dx (sinh x) = cosh x
d/dx (cosh x) = sinh x
d/dx (tanh x) = sech² x
sinh⁻¹ x = ln(x + √(x² + 1))
cosh⁻¹ x = ln(x + √(x² − 1))  for x ≥ 1

examples.setup: Show cosh²x − sinh²x = 1.
Differentiate y = cosh(x²).
steps: Chain rule. Outer cosh u → sinh u; inner u = x² → 2x.

common_mistakes: Putting a minus sign in d/dx (cosh x) (incorrect — it's sinh x without the minus).
Mixing up hyperbolic identities with trig (signs differ on the difference).
Treating sinh, cosh as periodic — they aren't.

lessons.title: 4.1 Maximum and Minimum Values
lessons.explanation: Definitions: f has an ABSOLUTE (global) maximum at c if f(c) ≥ f(x) for all x in the domain. It has a LOCAL (relative) maximum at c if f(c) ≥ f(x) for x near c. Similarly for minimums. EXTREME VALUE THEOREM (EVT): if f is continuous on a closed interval [a, b], then f attains an absolute max and absolute min on [a, b]. FERMAT'S THEOREM: if f has a local extremum at c and f'(c) exists, then f'(c) = 0. CRITICAL NUMBER: a number c in the domain of f where f'(c) = 0 or f'(c) does not exist. CLOSED INTERVAL METHOD for absolute extrema on [a, b]: (1) find values of f at critical numbers in (a, b); (2) find f(a) and f(b); (3) the largest is the abs max, the smallest is the abs min.
formulas: Critical number: f'(c) = 0 or f'(c) DNE (and c in domain)
Fermat's Theorem: local extremum at c with f'(c) defined ⇒ f'(c) = 0
EVT: f cts on [a, b] ⇒ absolute max and min exist
Closed Interval Method: compare f at critical numbers in (a, b) and at endpoints a, b

examples.setup: Find absolute extrema of f(x) = x³ − 3x + 2 on [-2, 3].
steps: Max value 20 at x = 3; min value 0 at x = -2 and x = 1.
examples.result: Abs max 20 at x = 3; abs min 0 at x = -2 and x = 1.

examples.setup: Find critical numbers of f(x) = x^(2/3)(x − 4).
steps: f'(x) DNE when x = 0 (and x = 0 is in domain).

common_mistakes: Reporting endpoints as critical numbers (they aren't, but check them too in CIM).
Stopping at f'(c) = 0; also check where f'(c) DNE.
Forgetting that critical numbers must be in the DOMAIN of f.

questions.prompt: Abs min of f(x) = x² on [-1, 3]?

questions.solution: Continuity on [a, b] is the hypothesis.

answers: Q4: Continuity on a closed interval.

lessons.title: 4.2 The Mean Value Theorem
lessons.explanation: ROLLE'S THEOREM: if f is continuous on [a, b], differentiable on (a, b), and f(a) = f(b), then there exists c in (a, b) with f'(c) = 0. MEAN VALUE THEOREM (MVT): if f is continuous on [a, b] and differentiable on (a, b), then there exists c in (a, b) with f'(c) = [f(b) − f(a)]/(b − a). Geometrically: the tangent line at c is parallel to the secant line through (a, f(a)) and (b, f(b)). Consequences: if f' = 0 throughout an interval, f is constant; if f' = g' throughout an interval, f − g is constant. The MVT is the foundation for many later theorems (l'Hospital, Taylor's theorem, the FTC).
formulas: Rolle's: f cts on [a,b], diff on (a,b), f(a) = f(b) ⇒ ∃ c ∈ (a,b) with f'(c) = 0
MVT: f cts on [a,b], diff on (a,b) ⇒ ∃ c ∈ (a,b) with f'(c) = [f(b) − f(a)]/(b − a)
f' = 0 on interval ⇒ f constant
f' = g' on interval ⇒ f = g + C

examples.setup: Verify MVT for f(x) = x² on [0, 2] and find c.
steps: f is a polynomial — continuous and differentiable everywhere.

examples.setup: Show that the equation x³ + x − 1 = 0 has exactly one real root.
steps: Existence: f(0) = -1, f(1) = 1; by IVT (continuous), root in (0, 1).
Uniqueness: if there were two roots a, b, Rolle's gives c with f'(c) = 0.
But f'(x) = 3x² + 1 > 0 for all x — no root. Contradiction.

common_mistakes: Applying MVT without checking continuity on closed interval and differentiability on open interval.
Saying c is unique — there can be more than one c.
Confusing average rate of change (MVT slope) with the derivative (MVT slope).

questions.prompt: MVT slope of f(x) = x² on [1, 3].
Find c by MVT for f(x) = x² on [1, 3].
MVT slope of f(x) = x³ on [0, 2].

questions.solution: Equal endpoints is the special hypothesis of Rolle's.

questions.prompt: If f'(x) = 0 for all x in an interval and f(0) = 5, then f(2) =

lessons.title: 4.3 What Derivatives Tell Us about the Shape of a Graph
lessons.explanation: FIRST DERIVATIVE TEST (for monotonicity & local extrema): if f'(x) > 0 on an interval, f is increasing there. If f'(x) < 0, f is decreasing. At a critical number c: if f' changes from + to -, f has a LOCAL MAX at c; if from - to +, LOCAL MIN; no change ⇒ neither. CONCAVITY: f is concave UP on intervals where f''(x) > 0 (cup shape ∪); concave DOWN where f''(x) < 0 (cap shape ∩). INFLECTION POINT: where concavity changes (and f is continuous). SECOND DERIVATIVE TEST: if f'(c) = 0 and f''(c) > 0, c is a local min; if f''(c) < 0, local max; if f''(c) = 0, test inconclusive — use first derivative test.
formulas: Increasing/decreasing test: sign of f'
First derivative test: sign change of f' at critical c determines local extremum
Concavity test: sign of f''
Inflection point: f'' changes sign and f continuous at the point
Second derivative test: f'(c) = 0, f''(c) > 0 ⇒ local min; f''(c) < 0 ⇒ local max

examples.setup: Analyse f(x) = x³ − 3x² + 2.
steps: Local max at x = 0 (f = 2); local min at x = 2 (f = -2).
f''(x) = 6x − 6. f'' = 0 at x = 1. f'' < 0 left, > 0 right.
Concave down on (-∞, 1), up on (1, ∞); inflection at (1, 0).

examples.setup: Apply 2nd derivative test to f(x) = x⁴ − 4x³.
steps: f''(x) = 12x² − 24x. f''(0) = 0 (test fails); f''(3) = 36 > 0 ⇒ local min.
For x = 0, use 1st derivative test: f' < 0 left and right ⇒ NOT extremum.
examples.result: Local min at x = 3 (f = -27); x = 0 is neither max nor min.

common_mistakes: Calling f''(c) = 0 a guaranteed inflection point — concavity might not actually change.
Forgetting to test critical numbers where f' is undefined.
Reporting local extrema at endpoints (those would be 'absolute', not local in the standard usage).

questions.prompt: Concavity of f(x) = x³ at x = 1?
If f''(2) = 5 and f'(2) = 0, then x = 2 is:
Inflection point x of f(x) = x³ − 3x² + 1.

questions.solution: Second derivative test fails when f''(c)=0.

lessons.title: 4.4 Indeterminate Forms and l'Hospital's Rule
lessons.explanation: INDETERMINATE FORMS arise when limit laws don't directly resolve a limit. The basic indeterminates are 0/0 and ∞/∞; others reduce to these: 0·∞, ∞ − ∞, 0⁰, 1^∞, ∞⁰. L'HOSPITAL'S RULE: if lim f(x)/g(x) gives 0/0 or ∞/∞, and f, g are differentiable with g'(x) ≠ 0 near a, then lim f(x)/g(x) = lim f'(x)/g'(x), provided the latter exists or is ±∞. The rule may be applied repeatedly. For 0·∞ and ∞ − ∞: rearrange algebraically into a fraction. For 0⁰, 1^∞, ∞⁰: take ln, find the limit of the log, then exponentiate.
formulas: L'Hospital: lim f/g of form 0/0 or ∞/∞ ⇒ lim f/g = lim f'/g' (if RHS exists or = ±∞)
∞ − ∞ → combine into single fraction
Powers 0⁰, 1^∞, ∞⁰ → y = f^g; ln y = g ln f; find lim ln y; exponentiate

examples.setup: Evaluate lim_{x→0} sin x / x by l'Hospital.
steps: Differentiate top and bottom: cos x / 1.

lessons.title: 4.5 Summary of Curve Sketching
lessons.explanation: Systematic checklist for sketching y = f(x): (A) DOMAIN; (B) INTERCEPTS — y-intercept f(0); x-intercepts solve f(x) = 0; (C) SYMMETRY (even/odd/periodic); (D) ASYMPTOTES — vertical (where f → ±∞), horizontal (lim_{x→±∞} f), slant if applicable; (E) INTERVALS OF INCREASE/DECREASE via sign of f'; (F) LOCAL EXTREMA from first or second derivative test; (G) CONCAVITY and INFLECTION POINTS via sign of f''; (H) SKETCH putting all the above together. Use this routine consistently and sketches become straightforward.
formulas: Vertical asymptote x = a: lim_{x→a} f = ±∞
Horizontal asymptote y = L: lim_{x→±∞} f = L
Slant asymptote: y = mx + b such that f(x) − (mx + b) → 0 as x → ±∞
Use signs of f' and f'' to characterise shape

examples.setup: Sketch outline of f(x) = x²/(x² − 1).
steps: Domain: x ≠ ±1. Intercept (0, 0). Even function (symmetry about y-axis).
Vertical asymptotes at x = ±1; horizontal asymptote y = 1.
f'(x) = -2x/(x²-1)²; positive for x < 0, negative for x > 0 ⇒ inc on (-∞, -1), (-1, 0); dec on (0, 1), (1, ∞).
Concave up on (-∞,-1), (1,∞); concave down on (-1, 1). No inflection points (the asymptotes interrupt).

examples.setup: Sketch outline of f(x) = x + 1/x.
steps: Domain x ≠ 0. Odd. Vertical asymptote x = 0.
Slant asymptote: f − x = 1/x → 0 as |x| → ∞, so y = x is slant asymptote.
f'(x) = 1 − 1/x². f' = 0 at x = ±1. Local min at x=1 (f=2); local max at x=-1 (f=-2).
f''(x) = 2/x³; concave up for x > 0, down for x < 0; no inflections (origin not in domain).

common_mistakes: Skipping domain and finding 'features' that aren't actually there.
Failing to check both directions (±∞) for horizontal asymptotes — they can differ.
Missing slant asymptotes when degree of numerator = degree of denom + 1.

options: Numerator degree exactly one more than denominator
questions.solution: Polynomial division gives linear quotient + remainder/denominator.
f(-x) = f(x) means reflection across y-axis preserves graph.

lessons.title: 4.6 Graphing with Calculus and Technology
lessons.explanation: Calculators and computer algebra systems (CAS) such as Desmos, GeoGebra, Wolfram Alpha, Maple, or Mathematica can plot functions instantly — but ONLY calculus reveals the important features (precise extrema, inflection points, asymptotes). Combined approach: (1) plot a wide window first to see overall behaviour and find approximate critical/inflection regions; (2) use calculus to find exact values; (3) zoom in to verify. Watch for: hidden behaviour at large scale (e.g. small bumps invisible in default windows), misleading near-vertical features that turn out to be very steep but finite slopes, and aliasing in highly oscillatory functions.
formulas: Computer plots SUGGEST features; calculus VERIFIES them.
Use multiple viewing windows (zoom in/out) to confirm behaviour.
For polynomials of degree n: at most n − 1 turning points and at most n − 2 inflections.

examples.setup: f(x) = x⁶ − 15x⁴ + 75x² − 125: how many real critical numbers and inflections?
steps: Zeros: x = 0 and x = ±√5 (each x = ±√5 is a double root).
Zeros: x = ±1, ±√5. So 4 candidate inflection points; verify each is a sign change.
examples.result: 3 critical numbers; up to 4 inflection points.

examples.setup: Why is graphing f(x) = sin(1/x) near 0 misleading?
steps: f oscillates infinitely fast near x = 0.
Any computer plot can only sample finitely many points.
The graph appears to fill a band rather than showing actual oscillations.
Calculus tells us lim_{x→0} f does not exist.
examples.result: Plots can mislead near essential singularities; calculus is decisive.

common_mistakes: Trusting a calculator window without checking whether features are present elsewhere.
Mistaking a steep but smooth curve for a vertical asymptote.
Reading approximate calculator critical numbers as exact answers.

lessons.title: 4.7 Optimization Problems
lessons.explanation: Optimisation = max/min in real settings. Strategy: (1) Read carefully; identify the QUANTITY TO OPTIMISE (the 'objective'). (2) Sketch a diagram. Assign variables. (3) Write the objective as a function of a single variable using a CONSTRAINT relating the variables. (4) State the domain. (5) Differentiate; find critical numbers. (6) Use 1st or 2nd derivative test to decide which is the optimum. (7) Check endpoints if domain is closed. (8) Answer the original question, with units.
formulas: Strategy: objective f(x) on a constraint g(x) = c reduced to single variable
Closed Interval Method or 1st/2nd derivative test for the optimisation
Always include units and check the answer makes physical sense

examples.setup: A rectangle is to be inscribed under the curve y = 4 − x² (with the base on the x-axis). Find the maximum area.
steps: By symmetry, vertices at (-x, 0), (x, 0), (x, 4 − x²), (-x, 4 − x²).

examples.setup: A box (no top) with square base must hold 32 m³. Minimise the surface area.
steps: Let base side x, height h. V = x²h = 32 ⇒ h = 32/x².
Surface area S = x² + 4xh = x² + 4x · 32/x² = x² + 128/x.
examples.result: Base 4 m × 4 m, height 2 m; min S = 48 m².

common_mistakes: Skipping the constraint, leaving the objective in two variables.
Forgetting to check endpoints/feasibility.
Reporting the value of x instead of the optimal value of the objective.

lessons.title: 4.8 Newton's Method
lessons.explanation: An iterative method for approximating roots of f(x) = 0. Starting from an initial guess x₀ near a root, the next approximation is x₁ = x₀ − f(x₀)/f'(x₀). In general x_{n+1} = x_n − f(x_n)/f'(x_n). Geometrically: at the point (x_n, f(x_n)), follow the tangent line to where it crosses the x-axis; that's x_{n+1}. The method converges quadratically (number of correct digits roughly doubles each step) when started near a simple root and f' ≠ 0 near it. It can fail (diverge or cycle) for poor initial guesses, or where f'(x_n) = 0.
formulas: Geometric: tangent line at (x_n, f(x_n)) intersects x-axis at x_{n+1}
Quadratic convergence near simple roots
Fails when f' = 0 at iterate, or initial guess far from root

examples.setup: Approximate √2 by applying Newton's method to f(x) = x² − 2 with x₀ = 1.
Approximate the root of cos x = x near x₀ = 1.
steps: f(x) = cos x − x; f'(x) = -sin x − 1.

common_mistakes: Using x_{n+1} = f(x_n) (forgetting the formula structure).
Choosing initial guess where f' = 0.
Stopping iteration before convergence and quoting unsettled digits.

lessons.title: 4.9 Antiderivatives
lessons.explanation: F is an ANTIDERIVATIVE of f if F'(x) = f(x). If F is one antiderivative, then every antiderivative on the same interval has the form F(x) + C for some constant C. Notation: the GENERAL ANTIDERIVATIVE includes '+ C'. Power: ∫ xⁿ dx = xⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) + C (n ≠ -1); ∫ x⁻¹ dx = ln |x| + C. Exponential: ∫ eˣ dx = eˣ + C; ∫ bˣ dx = bˣ/ln b + C. Trig: ∫ sin x dx = -cos x + C; ∫ cos x dx = sin x + C; ∫ sec² x dx = tan x + C. Inverse trig: ∫ 1/(1+x²) dx = tan⁻¹ x + C; ∫ 1/√(1−x²) dx = sin⁻¹ x + C. With initial values, antiderivatives are uniquely determined. Position from acceleration is two antiderivatives, requiring two initial conditions (typically initial position and velocity).
formulas: If F'(x) = f(x), most general antiderivative: F(x) + C
∫ xⁿ dx = xⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) + C  (n ≠ -1);  ∫ 1/x dx = ln |x| + C
∫ eˣ dx = eˣ + C
∫ sin x dx = -cos x + C;  ∫ cos x dx = sin x + C
∫ sec²x dx = tan x + C
∫ 1/(1+x²) dx = tan⁻¹ x + C;  ∫ 1/√(1-x²) dx = sin⁻¹ x + C
Linearity: ∫(af + bg) dx = a∫f dx + b∫g dx

examples.setup: Find the most general antiderivative of f(x) = 6x² − 4x + 7.
Find f if f''(x) = 6x and f(0) = 1, f'(0) = 2.

common_mistakes: Forgetting + C — antiderivatives are families, not single functions.
Trying to use the power rule on x⁻¹ — special case, gives ln |x|.
Sign errors: ∫ sin = -cos (note the minus).

topics.title: Integration: From Areas to the FTC

lessons.title: 5.1 The Area and Distance Problems
lessons.explanation: Two ancient problems motivate the integral. The AREA problem: find the area under the graph of y = f(x) above [a, b]. We approximate by dividing [a, b] into n subintervals of equal width Δx = (b − a)/n and summing rectangle areas: choosing sample points x_i*, the approximate area is Σ f(x_i*) Δx. Common choices: LEFT endpoints (x_{i-1}), RIGHT endpoints (x_i), MIDPOINTS. The exact area is the limit as n → ∞. The DISTANCE problem: given velocity v(t), find total distance travelled on [a, b]. Same structure: partition [a, b], approximate distance on each subinterval as v(t_i*) Δt, sum, take limit. Both problems lead to the same mathematical object — the DEFINITE INTEGRAL.
formulas: Δx = (b − a)/n
Right Riemann sum: R_n = Σ_{i=1}^n f(x_i) Δx with x_i = a + i Δx
Left Riemann sum: L_n = Σ_{i=1}^n f(x_{i-1}) Δx
Midpoint sum: M_n = Σ_{i=1}^n f((x_{i-1} + x_i)/2) Δx
Exact area = lim_{n→∞} of any of these (for continuous f)
Distance from velocity: d ≈ Σ v(t_i*) Δt

examples.setup: Estimate the area under f(x) = x² on [0, 2] using n = 4 right endpoints.
steps: (True area = 8/3 ≈ 2.667 — right endpoints overestimate for increasing f.)

examples.setup: A car has velocity v(t) = 3t² m/s. Estimate distance on [0, 4] using midpoint rule, n = 4.
steps: (True distance: ∫₀⁴ 3t² dt = t³ from 0 to 4 = 64 m.)

common_mistakes: Confusing left/right endpoint heights — wrong direction of error for monotone f.
Using Δx = (b − a)/(n − 1) (off by one).
Forgetting to multiply by Δx after summing heights.

questions.prompt: Right endpoint sum R₂ for f(x) = x on [0, 2].
Left endpoint sum L₂ for f(x) = x on [0, 2].

questions.solution: Right endpoint = max in each subinterval for increasing f.

lessons.title: 5.2 The Definite Integral
lessons.explanation: The DEFINITE INTEGRAL of f from a to b is ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx = lim_{n→∞} Σ_{i=1}^n f(x_i*) Δx, if this limit exists (and gives the same value regardless of sample-point choice). f is INTEGRABLE on [a, b] if the limit exists. Theorem: continuous (or at most finitely many jump discontinuities) functions are integrable on closed intervals. Geometric meaning: if f ≥ 0, ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx is the area under f. If f changes sign, it's the SIGNED area: areas above the x-axis count positive, areas below count negative. Properties: linearity, additivity over intervals, monotonicity, comparison theorems.
formulas: ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx = lim_{n→∞} Σ f(x_i*) Δx (if it exists)
∫ₐᵃ f(x) dx = 0;  ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx = -∫ᵦᵃ f(x) dx
Linearity: ∫(cf + dg) = c∫f + d∫g
Additivity: ∫ₐᶜ f + ∫꜀ᵇ f = ∫ₐᵇ f
Comparison: f ≤ g on [a,b] ⇒ ∫ₐᵇ f ≤ ∫ₐᵇ g
Bounds: m ≤ f ≤ M ⇒ m(b−a) ≤ ∫ₐᵇ f ≤ M(b−a)

steps: y = x is the line from (0,0) to (2,2).
Region under the line on [0, 2] is a right triangle with base 2, height 2.

common_mistakes: Treating definite integral as area when f changes sign (it's SIGNED area).
Forgetting the dx factor (Δx in Riemann sum).
Switching limits without flipping the sign.

Same upper and lower bound: zero.

lessons.title: 5.3 The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus
lessons.explanation: The FUNDAMENTAL THEOREM OF CALCULUS (FTC) connects the two pillars of calculus — differentiation and integration. PART 1 (FTC1): if f is continuous on [a, b] and g(x) = ∫ₐˣ f(t) dt, then g is differentiable on (a, b) and g'(x) = f(x). In words: differentiating an integral with variable upper limit gives back the integrand. PART 2 (FTC2, EVALUATION THEOREM): if f is continuous on [a, b] and F is any antiderivative of f, then ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx = F(b) − F(a). This turns areas into algebra: find an antiderivative, evaluate at the bounds, subtract. With FTC, definite integrals become tractable problems. Notation: F(b) − F(a) is often written F(x)|ₐᵇ or [F(x)]ₐᵇ.
formulas: FTC Part 1: d/dx ∫ₐˣ f(t) dt = f(x)
FTC Part 2: ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx = F(b) − F(a) where F' = f
Notation: [F(x)]ₐᵇ = F(b) − F(a)
With chain rule: d/dx ∫ₐ^{u(x)} f(t) dt = f(u(x)) · u'(x)

examples.setup: Compute ∫₀¹ x² dx using FTC2.
If g(x) = ∫₁ˣ √(t² + 1) dt, find g'(x).

common_mistakes: Forgetting the chain rule when the upper limit is a function of x (not just x).
Subtracting in the wrong order: F(a) − F(b) instead of F(b) − F(a).
Treating ∫ as a pure 'area' — it's signed, and FTC gives values directly.

lessons.title: 5.4 Indefinite Integrals and the Net Change Theorem
lessons.explanation: The INDEFINITE INTEGRAL ∫ f(x) dx denotes the family of antiderivatives F(x) + C. (Definite integrals are NUMBERS; indefinite are FUNCTIONS plus a constant.) Standard antiderivative formulas: ∫ xⁿ dx = xⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) + C (n ≠ -1); ∫ 1/x dx = ln |x| + C; ∫ eˣ dx = eˣ + C; ∫ sin x dx = -cos x + C; etc. NET CHANGE THEOREM: ∫ₐᵇ F'(x) dx = F(b) − F(a). The integral of a rate of change is the total change. Hence: total displacement = ∫ v(t) dt; total distance = ∫ |v(t)| dt; total cost change = ∫ C'(x) dx; etc.
formulas: ∫ f(x) dx = F(x) + C  where F' = f
Net Change: ∫ₐᵇ F'(x) dx = F(b) − F(a)
Displacement on [a, b]: ∫ₐᵇ v(t) dt
Total distance on [a, b]: ∫ₐᵇ |v(t)| dt
∫ xⁿ dx = xⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) + C; ∫ 1/x dx = ln |x| + C; ∫ eˣ dx = eˣ + C

examples.setup: Find ∫(3x² + 2/x) dx.
steps: Term by term: ∫3x² dx = x³; ∫(2/x) dx = 2 ln |x|.

examples.setup: v(t) = t² − 4 m/s on [0, 3]. Find displacement and total distance.
steps: For total distance, find when v = 0: t² = 4 ⇒ t = 2.
examples.result: Displacement -3 m; total distance 23/3 m ≈ 7.67 m.

common_mistakes: Confusing displacement (signed) with distance (always ≥ 0).
Forgetting + C on indefinite integrals.
Power rule on x⁻¹ — must use ln |x|.

lessons.title: 5.5 The Substitution Rule
lessons.explanation: Substitution (also called u-substitution) is the chain rule played in reverse. To compute ∫ f(g(x)) g'(x) dx, set u = g(x), du = g'(x) dx; the integral becomes ∫ f(u) du. After antidifferentiating, substitute back. For DEFINITE integrals: change limits as well: ∫ₐᵇ f(g(x)) g'(x) dx = ∫_{g(a)}^{g(b)} f(u) du. Strategy: pick u to be the 'inner function' of a composition; check that du differs from a piece of the integrand only by a constant multiple. Symmetry shortcut: ∫₋ₐᵃ f = 0 if f odd; = 2∫₀ᵃ f if f even.
formulas: Indefinite: ∫ f(g(x)) g'(x) dx = F(g(x)) + C, where F' = f
Definite: ∫ₐᵇ f(g(x)) g'(x) dx = ∫_{g(a)}^{g(b)} f(u) du
Pick u = inner function so du matches part of the integrand (up to a constant)
Symmetry: ∫₋ₐᵃ (odd) = 0;  ∫₋ₐᵃ (even) = 2 ∫₀ᵃ

examples.setup: Compute ∫ 2x √(x² + 1) dx.
steps: Let u = x² + 1; then du = 2x dx — exactly the rest of the integrand.

examples.setup: Compute ∫₀¹ x e^(x²) dx.
steps: u = x²; du = 2x dx; so x dx = du/2.

common_mistakes: Forgetting to change the limits when doing definite integrals — or not converting back to x.
Choosing a u whose derivative isn't matched (up to a constant) by the integrand.
Sign errors when the constant multiple in du needs flipping.

lessons.title: 6.1 Areas Between Curves
lessons.explanation: The area of the region bounded above by y = f(x), below by y = g(x), and between x = a and x = b (where f ≥ g) is A = ∫ₐᵇ [f(x) − g(x)] dx (top minus bottom). If the curves switch which is on top, split the integral at the crossing points (where f = g). Sometimes integrating in y is easier: A = ∫꜀ᵈ [right(y) − left(y)] dy. Strategy: (1) sketch; (2) find intersection points by setting curves equal; (3) determine which function is on top in each interval; (4) integrate piece by piece.
formulas: Vertical strips: A = ∫ₐᵇ [f(x) − g(x)] dx,  f ≥ g on [a, b]
Horizontal strips: A = ∫꜀ᵈ [right(y) − left(y)] dy
If curves cross, split the integral
Always check 'top minus bottom' (or 'right minus left') sign

examples.setup: Find area between y = x² and y = 2x.

examples.setup: Area enclosed by y = x³ and y = x.

On [0, 1]: at x = 0.5, x³ = 0.125 < x = 0.5, so x on top.

Not splitting at intersection points where roles swap.
Using vertical strips when horizontal would be much simpler.

lessons.title: 6.2 Volumes
lessons.explanation: DISK / WASHER METHOD: a solid generated by rotating a region about an axis can be sliced perpendicular to the axis into thin disks (or annular washers). Volume of a thin disk: πR² dx (or dy). Volume of a washer: π(R² − r²) dx where R is outer radius, r inner. More generally, a solid with cross-sectional area A(x) on [a, b] (perpendicular to the x-axis) has volume V = ∫ₐᵇ A(x) dx. Strategy: identify the axis of rotation, sketch a typical slice, write A in terms of x (or y), integrate.
formulas: Disk method (rotation about x-axis): V = ∫ₐᵇ π [f(x)]² dx
Washer method: V = ∫ₐᵇ π ([R(x)]² − [r(x)]²) dx
Cross-sectional volume: V = ∫ₐᵇ A(x) dx
Slices about y-axis: integrate in y instead

examples.setup: Find the volume formed by rotating y = √x, 0 ≤ x ≤ 4, about the x-axis.
steps: Disks of radius √x. Cross-section A(x) = π(√x)² = πx.

steps: Curves intersect at x = 0, 2; outer radius 2x, inner radius x².

common_mistakes: Squaring AFTER subtracting: (R − r)² ≠ R² − r².
Forgetting the π factor.
Mixing up which curve is the outer / inner radius.

lessons.title: 6.3 Volumes by Cylindrical Shells
lessons.explanation: An alternative method to disks/washers when the region is rotated about an axis PARALLEL to the strips. A typical thin vertical strip of width dx, at position x with height f(x), rotated about the y-axis sweeps out a thin CYLINDRICAL SHELL of radius x, height f(x), thickness dx. Its volume is approximately 2πx · f(x) dx (circumference × height × thickness). Total volume V = ∫ₐᵇ 2π x f(x) dx (for rotation about y-axis, f ≥ 0). For rotation about other vertical lines, replace x with the appropriate distance. Shells are often easier when rotating about y-axis a region bounded by y = f(x) (so that expressing x as a function of y would be hard).
formulas: Shell method (about y-axis): V = ∫ₐᵇ 2π x f(x) dx
About vertical line x = c: V = ∫ₐᵇ 2π |x − c| f(x) dx
About x-axis (using horizontal shells): V = ∫꜀ᵈ 2π y g(y) dy
Choose shells when slices are 'parallel' to the axis of rotation

examples.setup: Region under y = x² on [0, 2] rotated about the y-axis.
steps: Shell radius = x; shell height = x².
V = ∫₀² 2π x · x² dx = 2π ∫₀² x³ dx = 2π · 4 = 8π.

examples.setup: Region between y = x and y = x² on [0, 1] rotated about y-axis.
steps: Shell radius = x; shell height = (x − x²) (top minus bottom).
V = ∫₀¹ 2π x (x − x²) dx = 2π ∫₀¹ (x² − x³) dx.

common_mistakes: Forgetting the factor 2π.
Using x as both radius and height (height should be f(x), radius is x).
Reversing the height (top minus bottom).

lessons.title: 6.4 Work
lessons.explanation: WORK done by a constant force F over a displacement d (in the direction of the force) is W = Fd. For a variable force F(x) acting along the x-axis from a to b: W = ∫ₐᵇ F(x) dx. SI unit: joule (J) = N · m. Common applications: (i) STRETCHING A SPRING: Hooke's Law F(x) = kx (x = displacement from natural length). Work to stretch from a to b: ∫ₐᵇ kx dx = k(b² − a²)/2. (ii) PUMPING WATER: slice the volume horizontally. A thin slab of water at height y has weight ρg · A(y) · dy and is lifted (h − y), so work to empty = ∫ ρg A(y)(h − y) dy. (iii) LIFTING A HANGING CHAIN/CABLE: similar setup.
formulas: Constant force: W = F d
Variable force on [a, b]: W = ∫ₐᵇ F(x) dx
Hooke's Law: F(x) = kx; spring work from a to b: k(b² − a²)/2
Pumping fluid from a tank: W = ∫ ρg A(y) (h − y) dy (h = height to pump to)
Density of water (SI): ρ ≈ 1000 kg/m³; g ≈ 9.8 m/s²

examples.setup: A spring has natural length 0.3 m. Force of 6 N stretches it to 0.4 m. Find work to stretch from 0.4 m to 0.5 m.
steps: Stretching from 0.3 to 0.4: x = 0.1 m gives F = 6 N. So k = 60 N/m.

examples.setup: A cylindrical tank (radius 2 m, height 5 m) is full of water. Find work to pump all water to the top.
steps: Slice horizontally at height y, thickness dy. Slab area = π(2)² = 4π.
Slab volume = 4π dy; weight = ρg · 4π dy with ρg = 9800 N/m³.

common_mistakes: Using constant-force formula when force varies.
Forgetting to include the lifting distance (h − y) in pumping problems.
Confusing weight (ρg V) with mass (ρ V).

lessons.title: 6.5 Average Value of a Function
lessons.explanation: The AVERAGE VALUE of f on [a, b] is f_avg = (1/(b − a)) ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx. Reasoning: average of n samples = (Σ f(x_i*))/n; multiply and divide by Δx = (b−a)/n to get a Riemann sum divided by (b − a), and pass to the limit. MEAN VALUE THEOREM FOR INTEGRALS: if f is continuous on [a, b], there exists c in [a, b] such that f(c) = f_avg, i.e. ∫ₐᵇ f = f(c)(b − a). Geometrically: a rectangle of width (b − a) and height f(c) has the same area as the region under f.
formulas: Average value: f_avg = (1/(b − a)) ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx
MVT for integrals: f cts on [a, b] ⇒ ∃ c ∈ [a, b] with f(c) = f_avg
Equivalent: ∫ₐᵇ f = f(c)(b − a)

examples.setup: Average value of f(x) = x² on [0, 3].
Find c in [0, 3] with f(c) = f_avg from previous example.

common_mistakes: Omitting the 1/(b − a) factor.
Confusing average value with average rate of change.
Not checking c lies in the interval.

questions.prompt: Average of f(x) = sin x on [0, π].
Find c in [0, 4] with f(c) = 2 if f(x) = x.

lessons.title: 7.1 Integration by Parts
lessons.explanation: Integration by parts is the product rule played in reverse. From d/dx(uv) = u'v + uv', we get ∫ u dv = uv − ∫ v du. The trick is choosing u and dv from the integrand. MNEMONIC 'LIATE' — pick u in this priority order: Logarithmic, Inverse trig, Algebraic (polynomial), Trigonometric, Exponential. Then dv is whatever remains. Compute du by differentiating; v by antidifferentiating dv. Sometimes apply parts twice — and watch for an integral that returns to the original (set up an equation and solve).
formulas: ∫ u dv = uv − ∫ v du
Definite: ∫ₐᵇ u dv = [uv]ₐᵇ − ∫ₐᵇ v du
LIATE: pick u = first match in (Log, Inv-trig, Algebraic, Trig, Exp)
If integral returns to itself: get an equation for I and solve

examples.setup: Compute ∫ x eˣ dx.
steps: u = x (Algebraic), dv = eˣ dx; du = dx, v = eˣ.
∫ x eˣ dx = x eˣ − ∫ eˣ dx = x eˣ − eˣ + C.

examples.setup: Compute ∫ ln x dx.
steps: u = ln x (Logarithmic), dv = dx; du = (1/x) dx, v = x.
∫ ln x dx = x ln x − ∫ x · (1/x) dx = x ln x − x + C.

common_mistakes: Misplacing the minus sign: ∫ u dv = uv − ∫ v du, not + ∫ v du.
Choosing u/dv with no improvement (the new integral should be simpler).
Forgetting + C on the final answer.

questions.answer: x sin x + cos x + C
questions.solution: u = x, dv = cos x dx; v = sin x; result x sin x − ∫ sin x dx.
u = x, dv = sin x; v = -cos x.
u = ln x, dv = x dx; v = x²/2.
Apply parts twice to reduce polynomial degree.

questions.prompt: LIATE means pick u first in this category:
options: Logarithmic before inverse trig before algebraic before trig before exp

answers: Q1: x sin x + cos x + C.
Q2: -x cos x + sin x + C.

lessons.title: 7.2 Trigonometric Integrals
lessons.explanation: Strategies for integrating powers and products of trig functions. (i) ∫ sinᵐ x cosⁿ x dx: if m or n is ODD, save one factor of that function and convert the rest using sin² + cos² = 1; substitute. If both EVEN, use half-angle: sin²x = (1 − cos 2x)/2, cos²x = (1 + cos 2x)/2. (ii) ∫ tanᵐ x secⁿ x dx: if n even, save sec²x and convert rest with sec² = 1 + tan²; if m odd, save sec x tan x and convert. (iii) Products like ∫ sin(mx) cos(nx) dx: use product-to-sum identities.
formulas: sin²x + cos²x = 1
sin²x = (1 − cos 2x)/2;  cos²x = (1 + cos 2x)/2
1 + tan²x = sec²x;  1 + cot²x = csc²x
Product to sum: sin A cos B = (1/2)[sin(A−B) + sin(A+B)]
∫ sec x dx = ln |sec x + tan x| + C

examples.setup: ∫ sin³x cos²x dx (sin power is odd).
steps: Save one sin: sin³x = sin²x · sin x = (1 − cos²x) sin x.
Substitute u = cos x; du = -sin x dx.
Integral = -∫ (1 − u²) u² du = -∫ (u² − u⁴) du = -u³/3 + u⁵/5 + C.

examples.setup: ∫ sin²x dx using half-angle.

common_mistakes: Splitting an even power and substituting (no leftover factor of the right kind).
Forgetting the half-angle identity when both powers are even.
Sign errors when substituting du = -sin x dx.

questions.solution: Or ln|sec x|+C; substitution u = cos x.

questions.prompt: For ∫ sin⁵x dx, the easiest method:
options: Save one sin x, substitute u = cos x
questions.solution: Odd sin power: convert via sin² = 1 − cos².

lessons.title: 7.3 Trigonometric Substitution
lessons.explanation: When the integrand contains √(a² − x²), √(a² + x²), or √(x² − a²), trig substitution removes the radical. (i) For √(a² − x²): let x = a sin θ, dx = a cos θ dθ; √(a² − x²) = a cos θ. (ii) For √(a² + x²): x = a tan θ; √(a² + x²) = a sec θ. (iii) For √(x² − a²): x = a sec θ; √(x² − a²) = a |tan θ|. After substitution, the integral is purely trigonometric — apply earlier techniques. To return to x, draw a right triangle with the substitution geometry to read off sin θ, cos θ, etc., in terms of x.
formulas: √(a² − x²): let x = a sin θ;  dx = a cos θ dθ
√(a² + x²): let x = a tan θ;  dx = a sec² θ dθ
√(x² − a²): let x = a sec θ;  dx = a sec θ tan θ dθ
Use right-triangle geometry to convert back to x at the end

steps: Substitution x = sin θ; dx = cos θ dθ; √(1 − x²) = cos θ.
Integral = ∫ cos θ dθ / cos θ = ∫ dθ = θ + C.
x = tan θ; dx = sec² θ dθ; x² + 1 = sec²θ.
Integral = ∫ sec²θ dθ / sec²θ = θ + C.

common_mistakes: Choosing the wrong trig substitution for the radical type.
Forgetting to update dx and bounds (for definite integrals).
Failing to convert θ back to x at the end.

questions.solution: x = 2 sin θ; integral becomes θ + C.
x = sec θ leads to ln|sec θ + tan θ| + C.
x = sin θ; integral becomes ∫cos²θ dθ = θ/2 + sin(2θ)/4.

lessons.title: 7.4 Integration of Rational Functions by Partial Fractions
lessons.explanation: Any RATIONAL function P(x)/Q(x) (with deg P < deg Q; otherwise long-divide first) can be written as a sum of simpler fractions. CASE 1 (distinct linear factors): 1/((x − a)(x − b)) = A/(x − a) + B/(x − b). CASE 2 (repeated linear): include A/(x − a) + B/(x − a)² + ... up to the multiplicity. CASE 3 (irreducible quadratic ax² + bx + c): (Ax + B)/(ax² + bx + c). CASE 4 (repeated irreducible quadratic): include increasing powers. Integrate each piece: 1/(x − a) → ln |x − a|; 1/(x²+a²) → (1/a) tan⁻¹(x/a); etc.
formulas: Distinct linear: P/Q = Σ A_i/(x − a_i)
Repeated linear (x − a)^k: terms A_1/(x−a) + A_2/(x−a)² + ... + A_k/(x−a)^k
Irreducible quadratic: (Ax + B)/(ax² + bx + c)
∫ dx/(x − a) = ln |x − a| + C
∫ dx/(x² + a²) = (1/a) tan⁻¹(x/a) + C

examples.setup: Decompose 1/(x² − 1) and integrate.

steps: First part: u = x²+1 substitution gives (5/2) ln(x² + 1).

common_mistakes: Forgetting to long-divide first if deg P ≥ deg Q.
Missing the higher-power terms when there's a repeated factor.
Putting (Ax + B) over (x − a)² instead of A/(x−a) + B/(x − a)².

questions.prompt: Partial fractions of 1/(x(x − 1)): A/x + B/(x − 1). Find A.

lessons.title: 7.5 Strategy for Integration
lessons.explanation: There's no single 'right' method for every integral. Suggested workflow: (1) SIMPLIFY ALGEBRAICALLY — expand, combine fractions, use trig identities. (2) Look for an OBVIOUS u-SUBSTITUTION — does the derivative of some inner function appear (up to a constant)? (3) Classify the integrand: rational → partial fractions; trig powers → identities or save-one-substitute strategy; product algebraic·trig/exp → integration by parts; radical of √(a²±x²) or √(x²−a²) → trig substitution. (4) If standard techniques fail, try a different substitution or rationalising. (5) Some integrals have NO elementary antiderivative (∫ e^(-x²) dx is the prime example) — use numerical methods or special functions.
formulas: Mental flowchart: simplify → substitution → classify → parts/trig sub → consult tables
Forms with no elementary antiderivative: ∫ e^(-x²), ∫ sin(x²), ∫ (sin x)/x, ∫ 1/ln x
Always check that the derivative of your answer matches the integrand

examples.setup: ∫ x √(x² + 1) dx — choose method.
steps: Inner function x² + 1 has derivative 2x; integrand has x — perfect for substitution.

examples.setup: ∫ x ln x dx — choose method.
steps: Algebraic·log: integration by parts (LIATE — pick u = ln x).
u = ln x, dv = x dx; v = x²/2; du = dx/x.
Result: (x²/2) ln x − (1/2) ∫ x dx = (x²/2) ln x − x²/4 + C.

common_mistakes: Reaching for parts/trig sub before trying simple substitution.
Not simplifying first — many integrals melt away with a trig identity.
Believing every integral has an elementary form.

questions.solution: It does not (related to the error function).
Radical √(a² − x²) ⇒ x = a sin θ.

lessons.title: 7.6 Integration Using Tables and Technology
lessons.explanation: Mathematicians have compiled extensive tables of integrals (and modern CAS — Mathematica, Maple, SymPy, Wolfram Alpha — encode them). Use a table by: identifying the form of the integrand, finding a matching entry, applying it (sometimes with a substitution to match shape). Tables save time but you must understand WHICH technique would derive each entry. CAS can integrate symbolically; results may differ in form from yours by a constant. CAS also handles definite integrals numerically. Be aware: CAS sometimes returns answers in terms of special functions or unsimplified forms.
formulas: Table entries are templates; match shape and apply
When CAS gives ∫ f = G(x), CHECK by differentiating G
CAS results often need algebraic simplification
Differences from your answer are typically a constant (acceptable)

examples.setup: Use a table entry ∫ √(a² + u²) du = (u/2)√(a²+u²) + (a²/2) ln(u + √(a²+u²)) + C to compute ∫ √(x² + 9) dx.
Numerical CAS check: ∫₀¹ e^(-x²) dx ≈ 0.7468 (using Simpson's or computer).
steps: Numerical methods (n = 10 midpoint or Simpson's) give ≈ 0.7468.
True value (in terms of error function): (√π/2) erf(1).

common_mistakes: Using a table entry without checking that the form actually matches.
Assuming the CAS answer is simplest or most useful.
Not verifying CAS answers by differentiation.

questions.prompt: If a CAS gives a different answer than yours, the issue is most likely:
Best way to verify a complicated antiderivative:
If a table integral has variable u and your problem has 2x:

questions.solution: Many integrals require special functions like erf, Si, Li.

lessons.title: 7.7 Approximate Integration
lessons.explanation: When an antiderivative is unavailable or too complex, NUMERICAL methods estimate ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx. With n equal subintervals of width Δx = (b − a)/n: (i) MIDPOINT: M_n = Δx · Σ f((x_{i-1} + x_i)/2). (ii) TRAPEZOIDAL: T_n = (Δx/2)(f(x₀) + 2f(x₁) + ... + 2f(x_{n−1}) + f(xₙ)). (iii) SIMPSON'S RULE (n must be EVEN): S_n = (Δx/3)(f(x₀) + 4f(x₁) + 2f(x₂) + 4f(x₃) + ... + 4f(x_{n−1}) + f(xₙ)). Error bounds depend on f'' (Trap and Mid) or f^(4) (Simpson). Simpson's is dramatically more accurate than Trapezoidal for smooth f.
formulas: Δx = (b − a)/n
Midpoint M_n = Δx · Σ f(x̄_i)
Trapezoidal T_n = (Δx/2)(f(x₀) + 2f(x₁) + ... + 2f(x_{n−1}) + f(xₙ))
Simpson S_n = (Δx/3)(f(x₀) + 4f(x₁) + 2f(x₂) + ... + 4f(x_{n−1}) + f(xₙ))  (n even)
Trap error bound: |E_T| ≤ K(b−a)³/(12n²),  K = max |f''|
Simpson error bound: |E_S| ≤ K(b−a)⁵/(180n⁴),  K = max |f^(4)|

examples.setup: Approximate ∫₀² 1/(1 + x²) dx using Simpson's S_4.
Trapezoidal T_4 for the same integral.

common_mistakes: Misapplying weights in Simpson's (alternating 4-2-4-2-... starting and ending with 1).
Using Simpson's with odd n (requires even n).
Forgetting to multiply by Δx/3 (or Δx/2 for trapezoidal).

questions.prompt: Trapezoidal T₂ for ∫₀² x² dx (true value 8/3).
Simpson's is exact for polynomials of degree ≤:
questions.solution: Cubic exactness — fits quadratic to each pair, but errors cancel up through cubics.

lessons.title: 7.8 Improper Integrals
lessons.explanation: An IMPROPER integral has an infinite limit of integration or an unbounded integrand. TYPE 1 (infinite interval): ∫ₐ^∞ f dx = lim_{t→∞} ∫ₐᵗ f dx, if the limit exists (then we say it CONVERGES); otherwise DIVERGES. Similarly for ∫₋∞ᵇ and ∫₋∞^∞ (split at any point). TYPE 2 (discontinuous integrand): if f is unbounded at the upper limit b, ∫ₐᵇ f = lim_{t→b⁻} ∫ₐᵗ f. p-INTEGRAL test: ∫₁^∞ 1/xᵖ dx converges iff p > 1; ∫₀¹ 1/xᵖ dx converges iff p < 1. COMPARISON TEST: if 0 ≤ f ≤ g and ∫g converges, then ∫f converges; if ∫f diverges, so does ∫g.
formulas: ∫ₐ^∞ f dx = lim_{t→∞} ∫ₐᵗ f dx
∫ₐᵇ f dx with f → ∞ at b: = lim_{t→b⁻} ∫ₐᵗ f dx
∫₁^∞ 1/xᵖ dx converges ⇔ p > 1
∫₀¹ 1/xᵖ dx converges ⇔ p < 1
Comparison: 0 ≤ f ≤ g; ∫g cvg ⇒ ∫f cvg; ∫f div ⇒ ∫g div

examples.setup: Evaluate ∫₁^∞ 1/x² dx.
Evaluate ∫₀¹ 1/√x dx.
steps: Integrand → ∞ as x → 0⁺. Compute lim_{t→0⁺} ∫ₜ¹ x^(-1/2) dx.

common_mistakes: Treating ∞ as a normal upper bound and 'plugging in'.
Missing a discontinuity in the interior of the interval.
Saying a divergent integral 'equals ∞' as if a number.

lessons.title: 8.1 Arc Length
lessons.explanation: The length L of the curve y = f(x) on [a, b] (with f' continuous) is L = ∫ₐᵇ √(1 + [f'(x)]²) dx. Reasoning: a typical infinitesimal piece of curve has length ds = √((dx)² + (dy)²) = √(1 + (dy/dx)²) dx. For curves x = g(y) on [c, d]: L = ∫꜀ᵈ √(1 + [g'(y)]²) dy. Many arc-length integrands are messy (involve √(1 + (something)²)) — only special curves give clean closed-form answers; numerical approximation is often required.
formulas: L = ∫ₐᵇ √(1 + [f'(x)]²) dx
ds = √(dx² + dy²) = √(1 + (dy/dx)²) dx
Parametric: ds = √((dx/dt)² + (dy/dt)²) dt

examples.setup: Length of y = x^(3/2) on [0, 4].
steps: Substitute u = 1 + 9x/4, du = (9/4) dx; bounds u(0)=1, u(4)=10.

examples.setup: Length of y = ln(cos x) on [0, π/4].
steps: f'(x) = -sin x / cos x = -tan x. 1 + tan²x = sec²x.
L = ∫₀^(π/4) sec x dx = [ln|sec x + tan x|]₀^(π/4)

common_mistakes: Forgetting the +1 inside the square root.
Squaring f' incorrectly.
Using wrong variable bounds when switching x ↔ y form.

questions.prompt: Arc length of y = x on [0, 1].
Arc length of y = (2/3) x^(3/2) on [0, 3].
ds = ? for y = f(x) (in terms of dx).
Arc length of y = (1/2)(eˣ + e⁻ˣ) on [0, 1] = ?
questions.solution: 1+(f')²=cosh²x; √cosh²x=cosh x; ∫₀¹ cosh x dx = sinh 1 = (e − e⁻¹)/2.

lessons.title: 8.2 Area of a Surface of Revolution
lessons.explanation: When y = f(x) on [a, b] is rotated about the x-axis, the resulting surface has area S = ∫ₐᵇ 2π f(x) √(1 + [f'(x)]²) dx (for f ≥ 0). The factor 2π f(x) is the circumference swept out by a typical point on the curve at height f(x); ds is the arc-length element (slant length). For rotation about the y-axis (and curve described as y = f(x)): S = ∫ₐᵇ 2π x √(1 + [f'(x)]²) dx. Symbolically S = ∫ 2π (radius) ds, where the radius is the distance from the axis.
formulas: About x-axis: S = ∫ₐᵇ 2π f(x) √(1 + [f'(x)]²) dx
About y-axis: S = ∫ₐᵇ 2π x √(1 + [f'(x)]²) dx
General: S = ∫ 2π · (radius from axis) · ds
ds element: √(1 + (dy/dx)²) dx  (or √(1+(dx/dy)²) dy)

examples.setup: Surface area of y = √x on [1, 4] rotated about the x-axis.
Surface area of y = x³ on [0, 1] rotated about x-axis.
steps: Sub u = 1 + 9x⁴; du = 36x³ dx; x³ dx = du/36; bounds 1 → 10.

common_mistakes: Using 2π r instead of 2π f(x) (or 2π x for y-axis rotation).
Forgetting the ds (arc-length) factor.
Confusing surface-area formula with volume-of-revolution formula.

questions.prompt: Radius factor for rotation about x-axis: 2π · ?
Surface formula for sphere of radius r (rotate y = √(r² − x²) on [-r, r] about x).
Surface area of y = x on [0, 1] about x-axis.
