lessons.title: 8.3 Applications to Physics and Engineering
lessons.explanation: Integrals model many physical concepts. HYDROSTATIC PRESSURE / FORCE: force on a vertical submerged surface F = ∫ ρg · h(y) · w(y) dy (h = depth, w = width). MOMENTS AND CENTRES OF MASS: for a thin plate of constant density covering region R bounded by f and g on [a, b], moments M_x = (ρ/2) ∫ₐᵇ (f² − g²) dx, M_y = ρ ∫ₐᵇ x(f − g) dx; mass m = ρ · Area. Centroid: (x̄, ȳ) = (M_y/m, M_x/m). PAPPUS' THEOREM: volume of revolution = (centroid distance) × (Area) × 2π.
formulas: Hydrostatic force: F = ∫ ρg · h(y) · w(y) dy
Mass: m = ρ · Area
Moments (constant density ρ): M_x = (ρ/2) ∫ₐᵇ (f² − g²) dx; M_y = ρ ∫ₐᵇ x(f − g) dx
Centroid (x̄, ȳ) = (M_y/m, M_x/m)
Pappus' Theorem: V = 2π d · A,  d = distance from centroid to axis

steps: Set y = depth from water surface; gate extends y = 2 to y = 5; width 3.

Treating density as a function when it's constant.

lessons.title: 8.4 Applications to Economics and Biology
lessons.explanation: ECONOMICS: CONSUMER SURPLUS for a demand function p = D(q) at price P (with quantity Q such that D(Q) = P): CS = ∫₀^Q [D(q) − P] dq (area between demand curve and price line). PRODUCER SURPLUS: PS = ∫₀^Q [P − S(q)] dq (between price and supply). FUTURE VALUE of an income stream f(t) over [0, T] with continuous compounding rate r: A = ∫₀^T f(t) e^(r(T-t)) dt. BIOLOGY: BLOOD FLOW (Poiseuille): flow through cylindrical vessel = ∫₀^R 2π r · v(r) dr with v(r) = (P/(4η L))(R² − r²), giving F = πPR⁴/(8ηL). CARDIAC OUTPUT (dye-dilution): F = A/∫₀^T c(t) dt.
formulas: Consumer surplus: CS = ∫₀^Q [D(q) − P] dq
Producer surplus: PS = ∫₀^Q [P − S(q)] dq
Future value: A = ∫₀^T f(t) e^(r(T − t)) dt
Poiseuille flow: F = ∫₀^R 2π r v(r) dr;  with v(r) = (P/(4ηL))(R² − r²): F = πPR⁴/(8ηL)
Cardiac output: F = A / ∫₀^T c(t) dt  (A = amount of dye injected)

Forgetting to discount each contribution at its own time in present-value problems.
Using flow as v · area when v varies (Poiseuille's careful integration).

lessons.title: 8.5 Probability
lessons.explanation: A continuous random variable X has a PROBABILITY DENSITY FUNCTION (PDF) f if P(a ≤ X ≤ b) = ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx. f must be nonnegative, and ∫₋∞^∞ f = 1. The MEAN (expected value) μ = ∫ x f(x) dx; the MEDIAN m solves ∫₋∞^m f(x) dx = 1/2. Common distributions: (i) UNIFORM on [a, b]: f = 1/(b − a). (ii) EXPONENTIAL with parameter c: f(x) = c e^(-cx) for x ≥ 0; mean 1/c. (iii) NORMAL with mean μ and standard deviation σ: f(x) = (1/(σ√(2π))) e^(-(x − μ)²/(2σ²)). For normal, no elementary antiderivative — use tables or computer.
formulas: PDF: f ≥ 0, ∫₋∞^∞ f = 1; P(a ≤ X ≤ b) = ∫ₐᵇ f
Mean: μ = ∫ x f(x) dx
Variance: σ² = ∫ (x − μ)² f(x) dx
Uniform on [a, b]: f = 1/(b − a); μ = (a + b)/2
Exponential rate c: f(x) = c e^(-cx) on x ≥ 0; μ = 1/c

examples.setup: X ~ Uniform on [0, 4]. Find P(1 ≤ X ≤ 3).
X ~ Exponential rate 0.2. Find P(X > 5).

common_mistakes: Forgetting that PDF integrates to 1 over its full domain.
Treating the PDF VALUE as a probability (PDF is a density, not a probability).
Confusing PDF with CDF (cumulative).

questions.prompt: If f(x) = 2x on [0, 1], is it a valid PDF? (yes/no)
If X ~ Uniform[0,1], find P(0 ≤ X ≤ 0.3).

lessons.title: 9.1 Modeling with Differential Equations
lessons.explanation: A DIFFERENTIAL EQUATION (DE) is an equation involving an unknown function and its derivatives. ORDER = highest derivative present. A SOLUTION is a function that satisfies the equation on some interval. The GENERAL SOLUTION usually contains arbitrary constants (one per order). With INITIAL CONDITIONS we obtain a PARTICULAR SOLUTION. Examples of modelling: dy/dt = ky models exponential growth/decay (population, radioactivity). Newton's Law of Cooling dT/dt = k(T − T_env). Second-order: ma = F(x), e.g. simple harmonic motion m d²x/dt² = -kx. The art of modelling: translate a real-world rate statement into a DE.
formulas: DE: an equation involving y = y(x) and its derivatives
Order = highest derivative
General solution: family with arbitrary constants
IVP (initial value problem): DE + initial conditions → particular solution
dy/dt = ky: exponential model
Newton's cooling: dT/dt = k(T − T_env)

examples.setup: Verify y = e^(2t) solves dy/dt − 2y = 0.
Show y = sin(t) + cos(t) solves d²y/dt² + y = 0.
steps: y' = cos t − sin t; y'' = -sin t − cos t = -y.

common_mistakes: Confusing 'order' with 'degree'.
Forgetting that DEs typically have INFINITELY many solutions until an IC pins one down.
Plugging values too early — verify FIRST symbolically.

questions.prompt: If dy/dt = ky and k = 0.5, the general solution is y = ?
Does y = x² satisfy y' = 2y/x for x > 0?
If y' = 3y and y(0) = 4, find y(1).

lessons.title: 9.2 Direction Fields and Euler's Method
lessons.explanation: Most DEs cannot be solved in closed form. Two qualitative/numerical approaches: (i) DIRECTION (slope) FIELDS visualise dy/dx = F(x, y) by drawing short slope segments at sample points (x, y). Solution curves follow the field. They reveal long-term behaviour, equilibria, and stability. (ii) EULER'S METHOD numerically marches from an IC: given y(x₀) = y₀ and step size h, compute y_{n+1} = y_n + h · F(x_n, y_n) and x_{n+1} = x_n + h. It's a discrete approximation of following the slope field. Smaller h → more accurate (linear in h).
formulas: Direction field: at each sampled (x, y), draw segment of slope F(x, y)
Euler's method: y_{n+1} = y_n + h · F(x_n, y_n)
Step size h smaller ⇒ smaller error (O(h) per step)
Equilibria: F(x, y) = 0 (stable if neighbouring slopes point toward; unstable if away)

examples.setup: Use Euler's method (h = 0.5) to estimate y(1) for y' = x + y, y(0) = 1.
Identify equilibria of y' = y(1 − y).
steps: F(y) = y(1 − y) = 0 at y = 0 and y = 1.
Sign analysis: F > 0 for 0 < y < 1; F < 0 for y < 0 or y > 1.
y = 0 unstable (slopes point away above); y = 1 stable (slopes point in).

common_mistakes: Using x_{n+1} not x_n when computing F (use the OLD x).
Choosing h too large for accuracy.
Confusing equilibria of autonomous DEs with critical points of solutions.

questions.prompt: Euler step for y' = y, y(0) = 1, h = 0.1. Compute y₁.

options: Slopes of tangents to solution curves

answers: Q5: More accurate but more steps.

lessons.title: 9.3 Separable Equations
lessons.explanation: A 1st-order DE dy/dx = g(x) h(y) is SEPARABLE if it can be written so x's and dx are on one side, y's and dy on the other: dy/h(y) = g(x) dx. Then integrate both sides. (Where h(y) = 0, look for constant solutions y = c — these may be equilibrium solutions.) Final answer often has an arbitrary constant; apply IC if given to determine it. Examples: dy/dt = ky (separable; solution y = C e^(kt)); dy/dx = x/y (separable; y dy = x dx; y² = x² + C). Some answers are best left in implicit form.
formulas: Separable form: dy/dx = g(x) h(y)
Separate: dy/h(y) = g(x) dx
Integrate both sides: ∫ dy/h(y) = ∫ g(x) dx + C
Apply IC to find C; check for equilibrium solutions where h(y) = 0

examples.setup: Solve dy/dx = xy with y(0) = 2.
steps: Exponentiate: |y| = e^(x²/2) e^(C₁) = K e^(x²/2). Allow K real (sign absorbed).

examples.setup: Solve dy/dx = -x/y with y(0) = 3.
steps: Integrate: y²/2 = -x²/2 + C ⇒ x² + y² = 2C (a circle).

common_mistakes: Failing to recognize equilibria where h(y) = 0.
Dropping the absolute value when integrating 1/y (then losing sign info).
Forgetting + C until the end — apply ICs before isolating y.

questions.prompt: Separate dy/dx = e^x · y. Solution with y(0)=2: y = ?

lessons.title: 9.4 Models for Population Growth
lessons.explanation: EXPONENTIAL MODEL: dP/dt = kP gives P(t) = P₀ e^(kt) — unrealistic for large P (no carrying capacity). LOGISTIC MODEL: dP/dt = kP(1 − P/M), where M is carrying capacity. Solution P(t) = M / (1 + A e^(-kt)), where A = (M − P₀)/P₀. Properties: P → M as t → ∞ (stable); P = 0 unstable. Inflection point of P(t) at P = M/2 (fastest growth there). OTHER MODELS: Gompertz dP/dt = -k P ln(P/M); seasonal harvesting dP/dt = kP(1 − P/M) − h. Use phase-line / equilibrium analysis for qualitative behaviour.
formulas: Exponential: dP/dt = kP ⇒ P(t) = P₀ e^(kt)
Logistic: dP/dt = kP(1 − P/M)
Logistic solution: P(t) = M / (1 + A e^(-kt)),  A = (M − P₀)/P₀
Logistic inflection at P = M/2
Gompertz: dP/dt = -k P ln(P/M)

examples.setup: Bacterium population follows logistic with k = 0.5/h, M = 1000. P(0) = 100. Find P(t).
When does the population in the previous example reach 500?

common_mistakes: Forgetting carrying capacity M is a horizontal asymptote.
Using exponential model where logistic applies (and getting absurd long-term values).
Mis-deriving A from initial condition.

questions.prompt: If P satisfies dP/dt = kP and P(0) = 50, k = 0.1, find P(10).
In logistic, fastest growth occurs at P = ?
If A = 0 in logistic solution, P(t) = ?

lessons.title: 9.5 Linear Equations
lessons.explanation: A FIRST-ORDER LINEAR DE has form dy/dx + P(x) y = Q(x). To solve, multiply through by the INTEGRATING FACTOR μ(x) = e^(∫ P(x) dx). The left side becomes (μ y)'; integrate: μ(x) y(x) = ∫ μ(x) Q(x) dx + C. Then y(x) = [∫ μ Q dx + C] / μ. STRATEGY summary: (1) Get into the standard form. (2) Compute μ. (3) Multiply both sides by μ. (4) Recognise (μ y)' on the left. (5) Integrate. (6) Solve for y. (7) Apply IC.
formulas: Standard form: dy/dx + P(x) y = Q(x)
Integrating factor: μ(x) = e^(∫ P(x) dx)
Then (μ y)' = μ Q
Solution: y = (1/μ) [∫ μ Q dx + C]

examples.setup: Solve dy/dx + y/x = 1, x > 0.
steps: Multiply: x y' + y = x, i.e. (xy)' = x.

examples.setup: Solve y' + 2y = e^(-x), y(0) = 1.
steps: μ = e^(2x). Multiply: (e^(2x) y)' = e^(2x) e^(-x) = e^x.

common_mistakes: Putting equation in non-standard form (e.g. dividing wrong, sign of P).
Forgetting + C and then trying to apply IC.
Sign errors in computing μ when P is negative.

questions.prompt: μ for y' + (1/x) y = ln x.

options: y and y' appear linearly (no products like y·y')
questions.solution: Linear in y and its derivatives.

answers: Q4: Linear in y and y'.

lessons.title: 9.6 Predator-Prey Systems
lessons.explanation: Couples 2 populations: rabbits (R, prey) and wolves (W, predator). Lotka-Volterra: dR/dt = aR − bRW (rabbits grow on their own but are eaten by wolves), dW/dt = -cW + dRW (wolves die without prey but multiply when feeding). Equilibria: (R, W) = (0, 0) (extinction) and (R, W) = (c/d, a/b) (coexistence). Phase-plane analysis: trajectories are closed orbits around the coexistence equilibrium — populations oscillate. Modifications: logistic prey growth, harvesting, refuges add stability.
formulas: Lotka-Volterra: dR/dt = aR − bRW;  dW/dt = -cW + dRW
Equilibria: (0, 0) and (c/d, a/b)
Conserved quantity: H = d R − c ln R + b W − a ln W
Trajectories closed orbits in (R, W)-plane

examples.setup: Find non-trivial equilibrium for dR/dt = 0.4 R − 0.001 RW, dW/dt = -0.2 W + 0.0005 RW.
steps: Set both = 0 (assuming R, W > 0): 0.4 = 0.001 W ⇒ W = 400.

examples.setup: Initial state R = 800, W = 200. Will populations grow or decline initially?

common_mistakes: Sign confusion in cross terms: prey eaten is -bRW, predator grows as +dRW.
Looking for asymptotically stable coexistence — for basic LV, orbits are closed (neutral).
Treating as separable single DE.

questions.prompt: Coexistence eq. of dR = aR − bRW, dW = -cW + dRW.
LV trajectories in phase plane are:
LV constant of motion involves: d R − c ln R + b W − a ln ?

lessons.title: 10.1 Curves Defined by Parametric Equations
lessons.explanation: A PARAMETRIC CURVE is given by x = f(t), y = g(t), with t in some interval (the PARAMETER). As t varies, (x, y) traces a curve. Parametric form is more general than y = F(x) — it can describe curves that fail the vertical line test (circles, loops, self-intersections), and it captures TIME or direction of traversal. ELIMINATING the parameter means solving one equation for t and substituting; this gives a Cartesian relation between x and y. Examples: x = cos t, y = sin t (unit circle, traversed anticlockwise); x = t, y = t² (parabola y = x²); CYCLOID x = r(t − sin t), y = r(1 − cos t).
formulas: Parametric: x = f(t), y = g(t)
Eliminate parameter: solve one equation for t, substitute in the other
Circle radius r centred at origin: x = r cos t, y = r sin t, t ∈ [0, 2π)
Cycloid: x = r(t − sin t), y = r(1 − cos t)
Direction of traversal matters — same curve can be parameterised differently

examples.setup: Eliminate the parameter: x = t + 1, y = t² − 1.
steps: From the first equation t = x − 1.

examples.setup: Sketch x = 2 cos t, y = 3 sin t, 0 ≤ t ≤ 2π.
steps: Ellipse with semi-axes a = 2 (horizontal) and b = 3 (vertical).
At t = 0 we start at (2, 0) and traverse anticlockwise.

common_mistakes: Losing direction information when eliminating the parameter.
Forgetting to restrict the Cartesian curve to the part actually swept by the parameter range.
Treating t as just another name for x.

questions.prompt: Cartesian form of x = cos t, y = sin t.
At t = π/2 on x = 4 cos t, y = 4 sin t, the point is:

options: A point on a rolling circle
questions.solution: Tracing a fixed point on a wheel as it rolls.

lessons.title: 10.2 Calculus with Parametric Curves
lessons.explanation: For x = f(t), y = g(t): dy/dx = (dy/dt)/(dx/dt) (chain rule), valid where dx/dt ≠ 0. Second derivative: d²y/dx² = d/dx(dy/dx) = [d/dt(dy/dx)] / (dx/dt). AREA under a parametric curve from t = α to t = β: A = ∫_α^β y(t) · x'(t) dt (careful with sign and direction). ARC LENGTH: L = ∫_α^β √((dx/dt)² + (dy/dt)²) dt. SURFACE AREA (rotation about x-axis): S = ∫_α^β 2π y √((dx/dt)² + (dy/dt)²) dt.
formulas: dy/dx = (dy/dt)/(dx/dt)
d²y/dx² = (d/dt[dy/dx]) / (dx/dt)
Area: A = ∫_α^β y(t) x'(t) dt
Arc length: L = ∫_α^β √((x'(t))² + (y'(t))²) dt
Surface area (about x-axis): S = ∫_α^β 2π y √((x')² + (y')²) dt

examples.setup: Find dy/dx for x = t², y = t³ at t = 2.
Arc length of x = cos t, y = sin t on [0, π/2] (a quarter circle).
examples.result: L = π/2  (matches arc of unit circle)

common_mistakes: Computing d²y/dx² as (d²y/dt²)/(d²x/dt²) — wrong; differentiate dy/dx w.r.t. t then divide by dx/dt.
Forgetting the absolute-value/direction in area formulas.
Plugging t into a Cartesian-form derivative.

questions.prompt: dy/dx for x = t, y = t².
Arc length of x = t, y = t² on [0, 1].
dy/dx for x = e^t, y = sin t at t = 0.
questions.solution: dy/dt = cos t, dx/dt = e^t; ratio cos t / e^t; at t=0: 1/1 = 1.
Differentiate dy/dx w.r.t. t, divide by dx/dt.

questions.prompt: Arc length integrand for parametric: √(?+?) dt.

lessons.title: 10.3 Polar Coordinates
lessons.explanation: POLAR COORDINATES locate a point P by (r, θ): r is the distance from the pole (origin), θ is the angle from the positive x-axis (anticlockwise). Conversion: x = r cos θ, y = r sin θ; r² = x² + y², tan θ = y/x. A point has many polar representations: (r, θ) and (r, θ + 2π) are the same; (-r, θ) = (r, θ + π). The pole is r = 0 with any θ. POLAR CURVES r = f(θ): circles, cardioids r = a(1 ± cos θ), limaçons r = a + b cos θ, roses r = a cos(nθ) (n petals if odd, 2n if even), spirals r = θ.
formulas: x = r cos θ;  y = r sin θ
r² = x² + y²;  tan θ = y/x
Many representations: (r, θ) = (r, θ + 2π) = (-r, θ + π)
Common curves:
Cardioid: r = a(1 ± cos θ)
Rose: r = a cos(nθ) — n petals (n odd) or 2n (n even)
Spiral: r = aθ

examples.setup: Convert polar (4, π/3) to Cartesian.
Convert Cartesian (-1, 1) to polar.
steps: θ in second quadrant (x<0, y>0). tan θ = y/x = -1, so θ = 3π/4.

common_mistakes: Using arctan(y/x) without accounting for quadrant.
Forgetting that r can be negative (with adjusted angle).
Mixing up which trig function is x or y.

lessons.title: 10.4 Calculus in Polar Coordinates
lessons.explanation: TANGENT to r = f(θ): use parametric form x = r cos θ, y = r sin θ. Then dy/dx = (dy/dθ)/(dx/dθ), where dx/dθ = r' cos θ − r sin θ and dy/dθ = r' sin θ + r cos θ. AREA in polar: the area swept by r = f(θ) from θ = α to β is A = (1/2) ∫_α^β [f(θ)]² dθ — note the 1/2 r². ARC LENGTH: L = ∫_α^β √(r² + (dr/dθ)²) dθ. Strategy: many polar problems become tractable once written in this form, especially areas of cardioids, roses, and circles.
formulas: dy/dx = (r' sin θ + r cos θ) / (r' cos θ − r sin θ)
Polar area: A = (1/2) ∫_α^β [r(θ)]² dθ
Polar arc length: L = ∫_α^β √(r² + (r')²) dθ
For overlapping or symmetric curves, exploit symmetry to halve the integral

examples.setup: Find the area enclosed by r = 2 cos θ.
steps: This is a circle (radius 1, centred at (1, 0)) — traced for θ ∈ [-π/2, π/2].

examples.setup: Find arc length of r = θ from θ = 0 to π.

common_mistakes: Using just (1/2) r dθ for area instead of (1/2) r² dθ.
Choosing wrong limits — need the θ-range that traces the curve exactly once.
Forgetting to add (dr/dθ)² in arc-length integrand.

questions.prompt: Area of r = 2 (a circle radius 2) over θ ∈ [0, 2π].
Area enclosed by r = sin θ for θ ∈ [0, π].
Arc length integrand for r = f(θ):
Area of cardioid r = 1 + cos θ.

lessons.title: 10.5 Conic Sections
lessons.explanation: Conic sections (conics) — curves obtained by slicing a double cone with a plane — are parabolas, ellipses, and hyperbolas. Each has a FOCUS-DIRECTRIX or FOCAL definition. PARABOLA: set of points equidistant from a focus F and a directrix line. Standard form (vertex at origin, axis vertical): x² = 4py (focus (0, p), directrix y = -p). ELLIPSE: |PF₁| + |PF₂| = 2a. Standard form: x²/a² + y²/b² = 1 (a > b ≥ 0). Foci at (±c, 0) with c² = a² − b². ECCENTRICITY e = c/a (0 ≤ e < 1 for ellipse). HYPERBOLA: ||PF₁| − |PF₂|| = 2a. Standard form: x²/a² − y²/b² = 1. Foci at (±c, 0) with c² = a² + b². Asymptotes y = ±(b/a) x. Eccentricity e = c/a > 1.
formulas: Parabola (vert axis): x² = 4py, focus (0, p), directrix y = -p
Ellipse: x²/a² + y²/b² = 1, foci (±c, 0), c² = a² − b² (a ≥ b)
Hyperbola: x²/a² − y²/b² = 1, foci (±c, 0), c² = a² + b²
Asymptotes of hyperbola: y = ±(b/a) x
Eccentricity: e = c/a (0 parabola edge, < 1 ellipse, > 1 hyperbola)

examples.setup: Find foci, vertices of x²/25 + y²/9 = 1.
steps: a² = 25, b² = 9 (a > b, horizontal major axis).

examples.setup: Find foci, asymptotes of x²/16 − y²/9 = 1.
steps: a² = 16, b² = 9. c² = a² + b² = 25 ⇒ c = 5.

common_mistakes: Using c² = a² + b² for an ellipse (it's a² − b² for ellipse, a² + b² for hyperbola).
Mixing up direction of major axis (depends on which denominator is larger).
Mis-identifying eccentricity ranges.

questions.solution: x² = 4py form is for vertical axis; y² = 4px has vertex (0,0), focus (2,0).

lessons.title: 10.6 Conic Sections in Polar Coordinates
lessons.explanation: Unified focus-directrix description in polar form. With focus at the origin, directrix x = d (vertical, to the right), and eccentricity e: r = ed/(1 + e cos θ). For directrix x = -d: r = ed/(1 − e cos θ); for y = ±d (horizontal directrix): r = ed/(1 ± e sin θ). The eccentricity e classifies the conic: e = 0 (circle), 0 < e < 1 (ellipse), e = 1 (parabola), e > 1 (hyperbola). This is extremely useful for ORBITS: Kepler showed planets follow elliptical orbits with the Sun at one focus.
formulas: r = ed / (1 ± e cos θ)  (directrix vertical, x = ±d)
r = ed / (1 ± e sin θ)  (directrix horizontal)
Classification: e < 1 ellipse, e = 1 parabola, e > 1 hyperbola
Kepler: orbits are conics with the focus at the centre body

examples.setup: Identify r = 6/(2 − cos θ).
steps: Standardise: divide numerator and denominator by 2:
Compare to r = ed/(1 − e cos θ) with e = 1/2 (so ELLIPSE) and ed = 3 ⇒ d = 6.

examples.setup: Sketch r = 4/(1 + cos θ).
steps: Opens to the LEFT (since +cos θ corresponds to right directrix; focus at origin, parabola opens AWAY from directrix).

common_mistakes: Misreading the form: must standardise to coefficient 1 in front of denominator's leading 1.
Forgetting the sign of the cos/sin term affects directrix orientation.
Conflating r with x or y.

questions.prompt: In r = ed/(1 + e cos θ), eccentricity is the coefficient of cos θ in the denominator. Find e for r = 3/(1 + 0.7 cos θ).
Kepler's first law states orbits are:
options: Ellipses with sun at one focus
questions.solution: Newtonian gravity gives elliptical bound orbits.

answers: Q5: Ellipses with sun at focus.

lessons.title: 11.1 Sequences
lessons.explanation: A SEQUENCE is an ordered list a₁, a₂, a₃, ... of numbers. Formally, a function from ℕ (or ℕ ∪ {0}) to ℝ. CONVERGENCE: a_n → L as n → ∞ if for every ε > 0 there exists N such that |a_n − L| < ε whenever n > N. If no such L exists, the sequence DIVERGES. Sequences may diverge to ±∞ or oscillate. Limit laws (sum/product/quotient) hold as for functional limits. THEOREM: if a sequence is monotonic and bounded, it converges. Common limits: lim n^(1/n) = 1, lim r^n = 0 if |r| < 1, lim (1 + 1/n)^n = e, lim n!/n^n = 0.
formulas: a_n → L means: ∀ε > 0 ∃N ∀n > N: |a_n − L| < ε
Squeeze theorem applies: if b_n ≤ a_n ≤ c_n and b_n, c_n → L, then a_n → L
Monotone Convergence: monotonic + bounded ⇒ convergent
lim_{n→∞} r^n = 0 if |r| < 1; diverges if |r| > 1; = 1 if r = 1; oscillates if r = -1

examples.setup: Find lim_{n→∞} (3n² + 1)/(2n² − n).
steps: Divide top and bottom by n²: (3 + 1/n²)/(2 − 1/n).

examples.setup: Show {a_n} with a_n = n/(n+1) is increasing and bounded.
steps: By Monotone Convergence, converges. Limit = 1 (compute directly).

common_mistakes: Confusing the sequence n/(n+1) with the series Σ n/(n+1) — sequence converges, series diverges.
Treating divergent sequences as infinite numerical 'limits'.
Forgetting that decreasing + bounded below also implies convergence.

lessons.title: 11.2 Series
lessons.explanation: A SERIES is the sum of a sequence: Σ a_n = a₁ + a₂ + a₃ + ... PARTIAL SUMS: S_n = a₁ + a₂ + ... + a_n. The series CONVERGES to S if S_n → S; otherwise DIVERGES. GEOMETRIC SERIES Σ a r^(n-1) converges to a/(1 − r) iff |r| < 1. TELESCOPING: many series have partial sums whose middle terms cancel. nth-TERM (Divergence) TEST: if a_n ↛ 0, the series diverges (necessary, not sufficient — 1/n → 0 but Σ 1/n diverges).
formulas: Series converges if partial sums S_n have a finite limit S = Σ a_n
Geometric: Σ_{n=0}^∞ a r^n = a/(1 − r) for |r| < 1
Telescoping: Σ (b_n − b_{n+1}) = b_1 − lim b_{n+1}
nth-term test: if a_n ↛ 0, then Σ a_n diverges
If Σa, Σb converge: Σ(a + b), Σ(c·a) also converge with appropriate values

examples.setup: Sum the geometric series Σ_{n=0}^∞ (1/2)^n.
Determine convergence of Σ_{n=1}^∞ 1/(n(n+1)) (telescoping).

common_mistakes: Believing nth-term test is iff (it's only one direction).
Mis-identifying a or r in geometric series (off by one).
Treating the sum as the term limit (Σ a_n vs lim a_n).

questions.solution: Geometric starting n=1: a = 1/2, r = 1/2; a/(1-r) = 1.
Famous divergent series — slow but unbounded growth.
Only the divergence direction is conclusive.

lessons.title: 11.3 The Integral Test and Estimates of Sums
lessons.explanation: INTEGRAL TEST: if f is continuous, positive, and decreasing on [N, ∞) and a_n = f(n), then Σ a_n and ∫_N^∞ f(x) dx either both converge or both diverge. Use it for series whose term comes from an integrable function. P-SERIES: Σ 1/n^p converges iff p > 1. REMAINDER ESTIMATE for the integral test: if Σ a_n converges with sum S, then ∫_{n+1}^∞ f(x) dx ≤ S − S_n ≤ ∫_n^∞ f(x) dx, giving error bounds for partial sums.
formulas: Integral Test: a_n = f(n), f cts/pos/dec ⇒ Σa_n cvg ⇔ ∫_N^∞ f cvg
p-series: Σ 1/n^p cvg ⇔ p > 1
Remainder R_n = S − S_n satisfies ∫_{n+1}^∞ f ≤ R_n ≤ ∫_n^∞ f
Σ 1/(n ln n)^p cvg iff p > 1

examples.setup: Test convergence of Σ_{n=1}^∞ 1/n².
steps: f(x) = 1/x² is continuous, positive, decreasing on [1, ∞).
By integral test, the series converges.

examples.setup: Test convergence of Σ_{n=2}^∞ 1/(n ln n).
steps: f(x) = 1/(x ln x) is positive, decreasing for x ≥ 2.
∫_2^∞ 1/(x ln x) dx: u = ln x ⇒ ∫_{ln 2}^∞ du/u = lim ln u → ∞.

common_mistakes: Forgetting the decreasing condition (integral test fails for non-monotone terms).
Confusing p-series and geometric series.
Concluding integral and series have same VALUE — only same convergence behaviour.

questions.prompt: Σ 1/(n²) converges; estimate the error using ∫_{n+1}^∞ 1/x² dx.

lessons.title: 11.4 The Comparison Tests
lessons.explanation: DIRECT COMPARISON: if 0 ≤ a_n ≤ b_n and Σ b_n converges, then Σ a_n converges. If a_n ≥ b_n ≥ 0 and Σ b_n diverges, then Σ a_n diverges. LIMIT COMPARISON: if a_n, b_n > 0 and lim a_n/b_n = c with 0 < c < ∞, then Σ a_n and Σ b_n both converge or both diverge. Used to test a series by comparing to a known one (often a p-series or geometric). The limit version is more flexible — it doesn't require term-by-term inequality.
formulas: Direct comparison: 0 ≤ a ≤ b, Σb cvg ⇒ Σa cvg; b ≤ a, Σb div ⇒ Σa div
Limit Comparison: lim a_n/b_n = L ∈ (0, ∞) ⇒ same behaviour
Useful comparators: p-series 1/n^p, geometric r^n, 1/(n ln n) etc.

examples.setup: Test Σ 1/(n² + 1).
Test Σ (3n + 5)/(n² − 4n + 1) using limit comparison.
steps: Σ 1/n diverges (harmonic), so by LCT, original series diverges.

common_mistakes: Comparing on the wrong direction (smaller vs larger).
Using LCT when limit is 0 or ∞ — those cases are inconclusive without extra info.
Forgetting positivity hypothesis.

questions.prompt: Limit comparison: lim a_n/b_n = c ∈ (0, ∞) means a_n and b_n behave:

questions.solution: ln n grows slower than n^(0.5); compare to 1/n^(1.5).

lessons.title: 11.5 Alternating Series and Absolute Convergence
lessons.explanation: An ALTERNATING series has terms that alternate sign: Σ (-1)^(n+1) b_n with b_n > 0. ALTERNATING SERIES TEST (Leibniz): if b_n is decreasing and b_n → 0, the series converges. ERROR ESTIMATE: |error after partial sum S_n| ≤ b_{n+1}. ABSOLUTE CONVERGENCE: Σ a_n converges absolutely if Σ |a_n| converges (which implies Σ a_n converges). CONDITIONAL CONVERGENCE: Σ a_n converges but Σ |a_n| diverges. Example: alternating harmonic Σ (-1)^(n+1)/n converges (to ln 2) but Σ 1/n diverges — so it's conditionally convergent.
formulas: Alternating Series Test: b_n decreasing, b_n → 0 ⇒ Σ(-1)^n b_n converges
Error estimate: |S − S_n| ≤ b_{n+1}
Absolute convergence: Σ|a_n| cvg ⇒ Σ a_n cvg
Conditional convergence: Σ a_n cvg but Σ|a_n| div

examples.setup: Test Σ (-1)^(n+1) / n by AST.
Test Σ (-1)^n / n² for absolute convergence.
steps: Σ |a_n| = Σ 1/n² is a p-series with p = 2 > 1 — converges.

common_mistakes: Applying AST without verifying b_n decreasing AND → 0.
Thinking conditional convergence implies bizarre behaviour OK in practice (rearrangements can change sums).
Believing every alternating series converges.

questions.prompt: Error bound for partial sum S₅ of alternating Σ (-1)^(n+1)/n.
If Σ |a_n| converges, then Σ a_n:
Alternating Series Test gives convergence requires:

lessons.title: 11.6 The Ratio and Root Tests
lessons.explanation: RATIO TEST: compute L = lim_{n→∞} |a_{n+1}/a_n|. If L < 1, Σ a_n converges absolutely; if L > 1 (or = ∞), diverges; if L = 1, inconclusive. ROOT TEST: compute L = lim_{n→∞} ⁿ√|a_n|. Same conclusion structure. When to use which: ratio test shines for series with FACTORIALS or n! / r^n type terms; root test for series with terms raised to the n-th power. Both fail for p-series (L = 1 in each).
formulas: Ratio test: L = lim |a_{n+1}/a_n|; L < 1 cvg, L > 1 div, L = 1 inconclusive
Root test: L = lim ⁿ√|a_n|; same
Best for: ratio with factorials or exponentials; root with n-th powers
Both inconclusive on p-series

examples.setup: Test Σ n!/n^n.
Test Σ (n/(n+1))^(n²) by root test.

common_mistakes: Misreading ratio: a_{n+1}/a_n, not a_n/a_{n+1}.
Concluding from L = 1 (inconclusive).
Forgetting absolute values for series with sign changes.

lessons.title: 11.7 Strategy for Testing Series
lessons.explanation: Suggested order to attack a series: (1) Term limit — does a_n → 0? If not, divergent (nth-term test). (2) Recognise standard form: p-series? geometric? (3) Compare to a known series (direct or limit comparison). (4) Alternating? Try AST. (5) Has factorials, exponentials, n! / r^n? Try ratio test. (6) Has terms of form (something)^n? Try root test. (7) Integral test for monotone-decreasing positive terms. Always check ABSOLUTE convergence first when terms have mixed signs.
formulas: Mental flowchart: nth-term → known shape → comparison → AST → ratio/root → integral
p-series and geometric are the canonical comparators
Ratio for factorials/exponentials; root for n-th powers
Integral test for f(n) where f is decreasing positive

examples.setup: Strategy for Σ n!/100^n.
steps: Factorials in numerator suggest ratio test.

examples.setup: Strategy for Σ 1/(n √n + 1).

common_mistakes: Always reaching for the ratio test even when comparison is faster.
Skipping the trivial nth-term test.
Trying multiple tests with the same conclusion (waste of time).

lessons.title: 11.8 Power Series
lessons.explanation: A POWER SERIES centred at a is Σ c_n (x − a)^n. The set of x for which it converges is an INTERVAL OF CONVERGENCE. There's a RADIUS OF CONVERGENCE R, with 3 cases: R = 0 (only at a), R = ∞ (all x), or R > 0 finite (cvg for |x − a| < R, div for |x − a| > R, endpoints checked separately). Use ratio or root test on |c_{n+1}/c_n| · |x − a| or ⁿ√|c_n| · |x − a| to find R. Always TEST ENDPOINTS x = a ± R separately — they may or may not be in the interval.
formulas: Power series: Σ c_n (x − a)^n
Radius of convergence R: |x − a| < R ⇒ converges absolutely
Find R: ratio test on |c_{n+1}/c_n| · |x − a| < 1, solve for |x − a|
Endpoints x = a ± R: test each separately
Term limit, comparison, AST as needed at endpoints

examples.setup: Find R and interval of convergence of Σ x^n / n.
steps: x = 1: Σ 1/n diverges. x = -1: Σ (-1)^n /n cvg by AST.

examples.setup: Find R for Σ x^n / n!.

common_mistakes: Reporting only R but not testing endpoints.
Sign error in (x − a) when a ≠ 0.
Forgetting to take absolute values in ratio test.

options: Series converges only at x = a

questions.prompt: Endpoint behaviour for Σ x^n / n at x = -1.

lessons.title: 11.9 Representations of Functions as Power Series
lessons.explanation: Some functions have natural power-series representations on intervals around centre a. The geometric series 1/(1 − x) = Σ x^n on |x| < 1 is the seed. From it, by SUBSTITUTION, DIFFERENTIATION, and INTEGRATION (term by term, valid inside R), we generate many series: 1/(1 + x²) = Σ (-1)^n x^(2n) on |x| < 1; integrating: tan⁻¹ x = Σ (-1)^n x^(2n+1)/(2n+1) on |x| ≤ 1; ln(1 + x) = Σ (-1)^(n+1) x^n / n. These representations give exact infinite polynomial forms of common functions.
formulas: Term-by-term diff/integrate inside the radius of convergence

examples.setup: Find a power series for 1/(1 + x).
steps: Substitute -x in geometric: 1/(1 − (-x)) = Σ (-x)^n = Σ (-1)^n x^n.

examples.setup: Power series for ln(1 + x).
steps: ln(1 + x) = ∫ dt/(1 + t) from 0 to x = ∫ Σ (-1)^n t^n dt.

common_mistakes: Differentiating/integrating outside R (the new series may diverge).
Forgetting +1 in exponent or +1 in denominator after integration.
Mis-shifting indices when restating sums.

questions.prompt: Differentiate Σ x^n termwise: result series for x · d/dx[1/(1−x)]?
questions.solution: Multiply by x: Σ n x^n / x · x = Σ n x^n.

questions.prompt: Series for tan⁻¹ x at x = 1 gives sum:

questions.solution: Inside the open disc of convergence.

questions.prompt: Radius of convergence of geometric Σ x^n.

lessons.title: 11.10 Taylor and Maclaurin Series
lessons.explanation: If f has all derivatives at a, its TAYLOR SERIES at a is Σ f^(n)(a) (x − a)^n / n!. When a = 0 it's a MACLAURIN SERIES. Many familiar functions equal their Maclaurin series on some interval: e^x = Σ x^n/n!; sin x = Σ (-1)^n x^(2n+1)/(2n+1)!; cos x = Σ (-1)^n x^(2n)/(2n)!. The BINOMIAL SERIES: (1 + x)^k = Σ C(k, n) x^n where C(k, n) = k(k-1)...(k-n+1)/n!, valid for |x| < 1. Convergence of f to its Taylor series requires the REMAINDER R_n(x) → 0 (Lagrange form: R_n(x) = f^(n+1)(c) (x − a)^(n+1)/(n+1)!).
formulas: Taylor series at a: Σ f^(n)(a) (x − a)^n / n!
Maclaurin = Taylor at 0
e^x = Σ x^n / n!  (R = ∞)
Binomial: (1 + x)^k = Σ C(k, n) x^n,  |x| < 1
Lagrange remainder: R_n(x) = f^(n+1)(c) (x − a)^(n+1)/(n+1)!

examples.setup: Find Maclaurin series for e^x.
steps: f^(n)(0) = 1 for all n. Series: Σ 1 · x^n / n! = Σ x^n/n!.

examples.setup: Use Maclaurin for sin x to compute sin(0.1) to 4 d.p.

common_mistakes: Forgetting the n! denominator (it's the alternating factor with the (x − a)^n).
Mixing up sin and cos series (different parity in exponents).
Believing every C^∞ function equals its Taylor series (some don't — flat-Taylor examples like e^(-1/x²)).

questions.prompt: Taylor coefficient at x = a, term n.
First three nonzero Maclaurin terms of sin x.
e^x evaluated at x = 1 via series, first 4 terms: 1 + 1 + 1/2 + 1/6 = ?

lessons.title: 11.11 Applications of Taylor Polynomials
lessons.explanation: TAYLOR POLYNOMIAL of order n: T_n(x) = Σ_{k=0}^n f^(k)(a) (x − a)^k / k!. T_n(x) approximates f near a; the bigger n, the better the approximation. ERROR (Taylor's Inequality): |R_n(x)| = |f(x) − T_n(x)| ≤ M |x − a|^(n+1)/(n+1)! where M = max |f^(n+1)(t)| for t between a and x. Applications: numerical computation of trig/exp/log values; physics approximations (small-angle pendulum sin θ ≈ θ; relativistic energy at low speed); deriving formulas (Newton's method as 1st-order Taylor).
formulas: Linear approx: T_1(x) = f(a) + f'(a)(x − a)
Quadratic approx: T_2(x) = T_1(x) + f''(a)(x − a)²/2
Taylor's Inequality: |R_n(x)| ≤ M |x − a|^(n+1)/(n+1)!,  M = sup |f^(n+1)|

examples.setup: Estimate sin(0.5) using T_3 (Maclaurin) and bound the error.
Show small-angle approximation sin θ ≈ θ via Taylor.
steps: For |θ| = 0.1 rad, error ≤ 0.005 — usable for many physical models.

common_mistakes: Off-by-one in the Taylor formula (k! denominator pairs with (x − a)^k).
Using a value of M that's too small.
Treating Taylor approx as exact at the centre — it's exact only at x = a (and matches f's derivatives there).

questions.prompt: Error bound coefficient denominator for R_n: ?
Linear approx of cos x at 0.
questions.solution: T_1 = cos 0 + sin 0 · ... wait, derivative is -sin 0 = 0, so T_1 = 1.

units.title: Vectors and the Geometry of Space

lessons.title: 12.1 Three-Dimensional Coordinate Systems
lessons.explanation: A right-handed 3D coordinate system uses three perpendicular axes (x, y, z). A point is P(x, y, z). DISTANCE between P₁(x₁, y₁, z₁) and P₂(x₂, y₂, z₂): d = √((x₂−x₁)² + (y₂−y₁)² + (z₂−z₁)²). EQUATION OF SPHERE centred at (h, k, l), radius r: (x − h)² + (y − k)² + (z − l)² = r². The three COORDINATE PLANES are xy, xz, yz; they divide space into eight OCTANTS. Surfaces in 3D include planes (linear equations), spheres (quadratic in symmetric form), and cylinders (no dependence on one variable).
formulas: Distance: d = √((x₂−x₁)² + (y₂−y₁)² + (z₂−z₁)²)
Sphere: (x−h)² + (y−k)² + (z−l)² = r²
Coordinate planes: xy (z=0), xz (y=0), yz (x=0)
Right-hand rule: thumb x, index y, middle z

examples.setup: Distance from P(1, 2, 3) to Q(4, 6, 3).
Equation of sphere through origin centred at (1, 2, -1).
steps: Radius = distance from centre to origin = √(1 + 4 + 1) = √6.

common_mistakes: Forgetting the sign convention when expanding (x − h)² etc.
Using 2D distance instead of 3D.
Thinking of axes as left-handed (sign confusion in cross products later).

lessons.title: 12.2 Vectors
lessons.explanation: A VECTOR has magnitude and direction. In 3D, a vector v = ⟨a, b, c⟩ from origin to (a, b, c). MAGNITUDE: |v| = √(a² + b² + c²). Vector from P₁ to P₂: ⟨x₂−x₁, y₂−y₁, z₂−z₁⟩. OPERATIONS: addition (componentwise), scalar multiplication (each component), subtraction (add the negation). UNIT VECTOR in the direction of v: û = v/|v|. STANDARD BASIS: i = ⟨1,0,0⟩, j = ⟨0,1,0⟩, k = ⟨0,0,1⟩, so v = a i + b j + c k. PARALLELOGRAM RULE for addition; physical applications include force, velocity, acceleration.
formulas: v = ⟨a, b, c⟩ = a i + b j + c k
|v| = √(a² + b² + c²)
Vector P₁P₂ = ⟨x₂−x₁, y₂−y₁, z₂−z₁⟩
Sum: ⟨a₁,b₁,c₁⟩ + ⟨a₂,b₂,c₂⟩ = ⟨a₁+a₂, b₁+b₂, c₁+c₂⟩
Scalar mult: c⟨a,b,d⟩ = ⟨ca, cb, cd⟩
Unit vector: û = v / |v|

examples.setup: Vector from A(1, 2, 3) to B(4, 0, 5) and its magnitude.
Find unit vector in direction ⟨3, 4, 0⟩.

common_mistakes: Confusing point coordinates with vector components.
Adding magnitudes instead of vectors.
Forgetting to divide by magnitude when finding unit vectors.

lessons.title: 12.3 The Dot Product
lessons.explanation: The DOT PRODUCT of u = ⟨a₁, b₁, c₁⟩ and v = ⟨a₂, b₂, c₂⟩ is u · v = a₁a₂ + b₁b₂ + c₁c₂. Geometrically: u · v = |u| |v| cos θ where θ is the angle between them. Hence θ = arccos((u · v)/(|u| |v|)). u and v are PERPENDICULAR iff u · v = 0. PROJECTION of v onto u: comp_u v = (u · v)/|u| (a scalar); proj_u v = ((u · v)/|u|²) u (a vector). WORK: W = F · d when F is constant force and d is the displacement vector.
formulas: u · v = a₁a₂ + b₁b₂ + c₁c₂
u · v = |u| |v| cos θ
Perpendicular ⇔ u · v = 0
Scalar projection: comp_u v = (u · v)/|u|
Vector projection: proj_u v = ((u · v)/|u|²) u
Work: W = F · d

examples.setup: Find angle between u = ⟨1, 2, 3⟩ and v = ⟨4, -5, 6⟩.
Project v = ⟨3, 1, 2⟩ onto u = ⟨1, 0, 0⟩.

common_mistakes: Forgetting that u · v is a NUMBER, not a vector.
Computing angle without dividing by both magnitudes.
Confusing scalar vs vector projection.

lessons.title: 12.4 The Cross Product
lessons.explanation: The CROSS PRODUCT u × v of two 3D vectors is a vector PERPENDICULAR to both, with magnitude |u||v| sin θ (the area of the parallelogram spanned), and direction by the right-hand rule. Component formula: u × v = ⟨u₂v₃ − u₃v₂, u₃v₁ − u₁v₃, u₁v₂ − u₂v₁⟩, computed as the determinant of the matrix [i j k; u₁ u₂ u₃; v₁ v₂ v₃]. PROPERTIES: u × v = -(v × u) (anticommutative); u × u = 0; (cu) × v = c(u × v); distributes over addition. AREA of parallelogram = |u × v|; AREA of triangle = (1/2)|u × v|. SCALAR TRIPLE PRODUCT u · (v × w) = volume of parallelepiped.
formulas: u × v = ⟨u₂v₃ − u₃v₂, u₃v₁ − u₁v₃, u₁v₂ − u₂v₁⟩
|u × v| = |u||v| sin θ
Direction by right-hand rule
Anticommutative: u × v = -(v × u)
Parallel ⇔ u × v = 0
Triangle area = (1/2)|u × v|
Volume of parallelepiped = |u · (v × w)|

examples.setup: Area of parallelogram with edge vectors ⟨1, 0, 0⟩ and ⟨0, 1, 0⟩.

common_mistakes: Using a sign chart for j wrong (it's NEGATIVE in expansion).
Treating cross product as commutative.
Reporting cross product as a number (it's a vector).

lessons.title: 12.5 Equations of Lines and Planes
lessons.explanation: LINE through point P₀ with direction vector v: VECTOR equation r = r₀ + t v; PARAMETRIC: x = x₀ + ta, y = y₀ + tb, z = z₀ + tc; SYMMETRIC: (x − x₀)/a = (y − y₀)/b = (z − z₀)/c (provided no denominator zero). PLANE through P₀ with NORMAL vector n = ⟨a, b, c⟩: n · (r − r₀) = 0, i.e. a(x − x₀) + b(y − y₀) + c(z − z₀) = 0, or ax + by + cz = d. DISTANCE from point P₁ to plane ax + by + cz = d: D = |ax₁ + by₁ + cz₁ − d|/√(a² + b² + c²). Two lines may be PARALLEL, INTERSECTING, or SKEW (non-parallel and don't meet).
formulas: Line: r = r₀ + t v
Parametric: x = x₀ + ta, y = y₀ + tb, z = z₀ + tc
Symmetric: (x − x₀)/a = (y − y₀)/b = (z − z₀)/c
Plane: a(x − x₀) + b(y − y₀) + c(z − z₀) = 0
Distance point to plane: D = |ax₁ + by₁ + cz₁ − d|/√(a² + b² + c²)
Distance point to line: |P₀P × v|/|v|

examples.setup: Line through (1, 2, 3) with direction ⟨4, 5, 6⟩.
Plane through (1, 2, 3) with normal ⟨2, -1, 1⟩.

common_mistakes: Mixing up direction vector with normal vector.
Mis-signing constants when going to symmetric form.
Treating skew lines as if they intersect.

questions.prompt: Direction vector of line x = 2t, y = 1 − t, z = 3 + t.
Two lines that don't intersect and aren't parallel are:
Distance from origin to plane 2x + 3y + 6z = 14 (use formula).

lessons.title: 12.6 Cylinders and Quadric Surfaces
lessons.explanation: A CYLINDER (in advanced sense) is a surface where one variable is missing from the equation; it's traced by a curve translated along the missing axis. E.g. y = x² in 3D is a parabolic cylinder (parabola swept along z). QUADRIC SURFACES are level sets of second-degree equations: ELLIPSOID x²/a² + y²/b² + z²/c² = 1; HYPERBOLOID OF ONE SHEET x²/a² + y²/b² − z²/c² = 1; HYPERBOLOID OF TWO SHEETS -x²/a² − y²/b² + z²/c² = 1; ELLIPTIC PARABOLOID z = x²/a² + y²/b²; HYPERBOLIC PARABOLOID (saddle) z = x²/a² − y²/b²; ELLIPTIC CONE x²/a² + y²/b² = z²/c². Use cross-sections (TRACES in coordinate planes) to identify and visualise.
formulas: Cylinder: variable missing from equation
Ellipsoid: x²/a² + y²/b² + z²/c² = 1
Hyperboloid 1 sheet: x²/a² + y²/b² − z²/c² = 1
Hyperboloid 2 sheets: -x²/a² − y²/b² + z²/c² = 1
Elliptic paraboloid: z = x²/a² + y²/b²
Hyperbolic paraboloid (saddle): z = x²/a² − y²/b²
Cone: x²/a² + y²/b² = z²/c²
Trace = intersection with coord plane

examples.setup: Identify x² + y²/4 + z²/9 = 1.
steps: All squared terms, all positive coefficients, equals 1.

examples.setup: Identify z = x² + y².
steps: Right-side: sum of squares; left-side linear in z.
Elliptic paraboloid (here circular paraboloid since coefficients equal). Opens upward.

common_mistakes: Confusing 1-sheet and 2-sheet hyperboloids — sign of squared term.
Treating saddle as a paraboloid (different curvature signs).
Mis-orienting the surface (which axis it opens along).

questions.prompt: Trace of x² + y²/4 + z²/9 = 1 in xy-plane (z=0).

lessons.title: 13.1 Vector Functions and Space Curves
lessons.explanation: A VECTOR FUNCTION takes a real input t and outputs a vector r(t) = ⟨f(t), g(t), h(t)⟩. As t varies over an interval, the tip of r(t) traces a SPACE CURVE. LIMITS are taken componentwise: lim r(t) = ⟨lim f, lim g, lim h⟩ if all components have limits. CONTINUITY at t₀ means each component is continuous at t₀. Vector parametric forms describe lines, helices, twisted curves, and trajectories of particles. The image of r is the same regardless of how we parameterise it (different parametrisations describe the same curve at different speeds).
formulas: Componentwise limits: lim r = ⟨lim f, lim g, lim h⟩
Componentwise continuity
Helix: r(t) = ⟨cos t, sin t, t⟩ (circular base, climbing in z)
Line through P₀ in direction v: r(t) = r₀ + t v

examples.setup: Sketch r(t) = ⟨cos t, sin t, t⟩.
steps: Project to xy-plane: x² + y² = 1, the unit circle (cos²t + sin²t = 1).
z = t increases linearly, so the curve climbs.
Result is a HELIX — circular spiral around the z-axis.

examples.setup: Find lim_{t→0} r(t) where r(t) = ⟨t² + 1, sin t / t, e^t⟩.
steps: Componentwise: lim (t² + 1) = 1; lim sin t / t = 1; lim e^t = 1.

common_mistakes: Treating r(t) as a scalar.
Computing limits without checking each component.
Confusing the curve (image set) with the parametrisation (mapping).

questions.prompt: r(t) = ⟨cos t, sin t, t⟩ is:

lessons.title: 13.2 Derivatives and Integrals of Vector Functions
lessons.explanation: DERIVATIVE: r'(t) = lim_{h→0} [r(t + h) − r(t)]/h, computed componentwise: r'(t) = ⟨f'(t), g'(t), h'(t)⟩. r'(t) is the TANGENT VECTOR to the curve at r(t); the unit tangent T(t) = r'(t)/|r'(t)|. DIFFERENTIATION RULES (derived from componentwise rules): sum, scalar mult, dot, cross, and chain rule all hold; e.g. (u · v)' = u' · v + u · v'; (u × v)' = u' × v + u × v'. INTEGRAL: ∫ r(t) dt componentwise gives a vector function plus an arbitrary VECTOR constant of integration.
formulas: Unit tangent: T(t) = r'(t)/|r'(t)|
Product rules: (u · v)' = u' · v + u · v'; (u × v)' = u' × v + u × v'
Chain rule: (r(u(t)))' = u'(t) r'(u(t))
Integral: ∫ r(t) dt = ⟨∫f, ∫g, ∫h⟩ + C  (C is a vector)

examples.setup: Find r'(t) for r(t) = ⟨t², sin t, e^t⟩.
steps: f'(t) = 2t; g'(t) = cos t; h'(t) = e^t.

examples.setup: Unit tangent at t = 0 for r(t) = ⟨cos t, sin t, t⟩.

common_mistakes: Differentiating |r(t)| as if r(t) were a scalar.
Forgetting the constant in cross-product rule (the order matters).
Confusing tangent vector with unit tangent.

questions.prompt: d/dt (u · v) where u, v vector-valued:

lessons.title: 13.3 Arc Length and Curvature
lessons.explanation: ARC LENGTH of r(t) on [a, b]: L = ∫ₐᵇ |r'(t)| dt. ARC LENGTH PARAMETER: s(t) = ∫_a^t |r'(u)| du; reparametrize by s for natural coordinates. CURVATURE κ measures how quickly the unit tangent T turns: κ = |dT/ds| = |T'(t)|/|r'(t)|. Equivalent formula: κ = |r' × r''|/|r'|³. PRINCIPAL UNIT NORMAL N = T'/|T'|; BINORMAL B = T × N; together (T, N, B) form the orthonormal Frenet frame. TORSION τ measures how the curve twists out of its instantaneous plane (advanced).
formulas: Arc length: L = ∫ₐᵇ |r'(t)| dt
Curvature: κ = |T'(t)|/|r'(t)| = |r' × r''|/|r'|³
Principal unit normal: N = T'/|T'|
Binormal: B = T × N
Frenet frame: (T, N, B) orthonormal

examples.setup: Length of helix r(t) = ⟨cos t, sin t, t⟩ on [0, 2π].
Curvature of unit circle r(t) = ⟨cos t, sin t⟩.
steps: r' = ⟨-sin t, cos t⟩; |r'| = 1; r'' = ⟨-cos t, -sin t⟩.
examples.result: κ = 1 (reciprocal of radius — circle of radius r has κ = 1/r)

common_mistakes: Forgetting absolute value (magnitude) in arc-length integrand.
Mixing up |T'| with |r'|.
Treating curvature of circle as 0 (it's nonzero — straight lines have κ = 0).

options: T, N, B (mutually perpendicular unit vectors)

questions.prompt: |r'| for r(t) = ⟨3 cos t, 3 sin t, 4t⟩.

lessons.title: 13.4 Motion in Space: Velocity and Acceleration
lessons.explanation: If r(t) is the position of a particle: VELOCITY v(t) = r'(t); SPEED |v(t)| = |r'(t)|; ACCELERATION a(t) = v'(t) = r''(t). DECOMPOSITION of a in terms of T, N: a = a_T T + a_N N where TANGENTIAL component a_T = d|v|/dt (rate of change of speed) and NORMAL component a_N = κ |v|² (centripetal — turning). For UNIFORM CIRCULAR MOTION (constant speed): a_T = 0, a_N = v²/r. Used in physics: Newton's second law F = m a relates forces to acceleration.
formulas: v(t) = r'(t);  speed = |v(t)|;  a(t) = v'(t) = r''(t)
Tangential: a_T = (d|v|/dt) = (v · a)/|v|
Normal: a_N = κ|v|² = √(|a|² − a_T²)
a = a_T T + a_N N
Uniform circular: a_T = 0, a_N = v²/r

examples.setup: Particle moves with r(t) = ⟨t², t³, t⟩. Find v(1) and a(1).
Decompose acceleration of r(t) = ⟨cos t, sin t, t⟩.

common_mistakes: Confusing tangential acceleration (changes speed) with normal (changes direction).
Forgetting that constant speed implies a ⊥ v.
Computing |a| as if it equalled a_T or a_N alone.

lessons.title: 14.1 Functions of Several Variables
lessons.explanation: A function of two variables: f: D ⊂ ℝ² → ℝ; we write z = f(x, y). Domain is a region in the plane; range is a set of real numbers; graph is a SURFACE in ℝ³. LEVEL CURVES f(x, y) = c (contours) help visualise — they're 'topographic lines' on the surface. For three variables: w = f(x, y, z); cannot graph in 4D, but LEVEL SURFACES f(x, y, z) = c help. Examples: temperature in space, pressure, density. Linear functions: z = a x + b y + c. Quadratic: z = x² + y² (paraboloid).
formulas: Two-variable: z = f(x, y), graph in ℝ³
Domain often determined by where f is real
Level curves: f(x, y) = c
Three-variable: w = f(x, y, z), level surfaces
Standard surfaces: z = x² + y² (paraboloid); z = √(x² + y²) (cone)

examples.setup: Find domain of f(x, y) = √(4 − x² − y²).
steps: Need radicand ≥ 0: 4 − x² − y² ≥ 0, i.e. x² + y² ≤ 4.
Domain: closed disc of radius 2 centred at origin.

examples.setup: Sketch level curves of f(x, y) = x² + y².
steps: f = c gives x² + y² = c.
Concentric circles centred at origin (only for c ≥ 0).

common_mistakes: Forgetting to specify domain restrictions for multi-variable functions.
Trying to graph functions of 3 variables in 3D (use level surfaces instead).
Confusing level curves of f(x, y) with horizontal slices of the graph.

questions.prompt: f(1, 2) for f(x, y) = x² + y².
Domain of f(x, y) = ln(x − y).

lessons.title: 14.2 Limits and Continuity
lessons.explanation: lim_{(x,y)→(a,b)} f(x, y) = L if f(x, y) → L as (x, y) approaches (a, b) along ANY path. If different paths give different limits, the limit DOES NOT EXIST. STRATEGY for non-existence: try paths y = mx, y = x², or polar substitution. STRATEGY for existence: convert to polar (x = r cos θ, y = r sin θ) and check r → 0; also use the squeeze theorem with bounds like |xy|/(x² + y²) ≤ |x| etc. Continuity at (a, b) requires lim f = f(a, b). Polynomials, rational functions (where defined), compositions of continuous functions are continuous on their domains.
formulas: lim_{(x,y)→(a,b)} f = L: same value along EVERY path
Path test for non-existence: different limits along y = mx, y = kx²
Polar conversion: r → 0
Squeeze: bound |f − L| → 0
Continuity: lim = f(a, b)

examples.setup: Show lim_{(x,y)→(0,0)} xy/(x² + y²) does not exist.
steps: Different paths give different limits ⇒ DNE.

examples.setup: Show lim_{(x,y)→(0,0)} x²y/(x² + y²) = 0.
steps: Polar: x = r cos θ, y = r sin θ.
f = r² cos²θ · r sin θ / r² = r cos²θ sin θ.

common_mistakes: Concluding limit = L from one or two paths agreeing.
Treating polar conversion as a proof when r-dependence still depends on θ.
Forgetting that continuity at boundary points requires checking values along ALL approaches.

questions.prompt: If two paths give different limits, the limit:
To establish a limit exists, one path is:

lessons.title: 14.3 Partial Derivatives
lessons.explanation: PARTIAL DERIVATIVE of f(x, y) w.r.t. x: f_x(x, y) = ∂f/∂x = lim_{h→0} [f(x + h, y) − f(x, y)]/h. Holds y fixed, differentiates as a function of x. Similarly f_y. GEOMETRICALLY: f_x(a, b) is the slope of the curve of intersection of z = f(x, y) with the plane y = b at x = a. HIGHER ORDER: f_xx, f_yy, f_xy = ∂²f/∂y∂x. CLAIRAUT'S THEOREM: if f_xy and f_yx are continuous, they are equal. Three or more variables: same idea, hold all others fixed.
formulas: ∂f/∂x: differentiate w.r.t. x, holding other vars fixed
f_x(x, y) = lim_{h→0} [f(x+h, y) − f(x, y)]/h
f_xy = (f_x)_y = ∂²f/∂y∂x
Clairaut: f_xy = f_yx (when both are continuous)
Higher: f_xxy etc.

examples.setup: Find f_x and f_y for f(x, y) = x³y² + sin(xy).
steps: f_x: treat y as constant. d/dx (x³y²) = 3x²y²; d/dx (sin(xy)) = y cos(xy).
f_y: treat x as constant. d/dy (x³y²) = 2x³y; d/dy (sin(xy)) = x cos(xy).
examples.result: f_x = 3x²y² + y cos(xy); f_y = 2x³y + x cos(xy)

examples.setup: Find f_xy for f(x, y) = x²y³.
steps: Check: f_y = 3 x² y², (f_y)_x = 6 x y² ✓ Clairaut.

common_mistakes: Treating y like x when computing ∂/∂x.
Mis-applying chain rule on composite functions of (x, y).
Forgetting that f_xy means differentiate w.r.t. x first, then y (some texts use the opposite convention; here Stewart uses f_x then y).

lessons.title: 14.4 Tangent Planes and Linear Approximations
lessons.explanation: TANGENT PLANE to z = f(x, y) at (a, b, f(a, b)): z − f(a, b) = f_x(a, b)(x − a) + f_y(a, b)(y − b). LINEARISATION: L(x, y) = f(a, b) + f_x(a, b)(x − a) + f_y(a, b)(y − b); f(x, y) ≈ L(x, y) for (x, y) near (a, b). DIFFERENTIAL: dz = f_x dx + f_y dy. Used for error estimation. DIFFERENTIABILITY: f is differentiable at (a, b) if the linearisation gives a 'good' (sub-linear-error) approximation; sufficient condition: f_x and f_y exist and are continuous near (a, b).
formulas: Tangent plane: z − f(a, b) = f_x(a, b)(x − a) + f_y(a, b)(y − b)
Linearisation: L(x, y) = f(a, b) + f_x(a, b)(x − a) + f_y(a, b)(y − b)
Total differential: dz = f_x dx + f_y dy
Diff'ble ⇒ continuous; sufficient: f_x, f_y continuous

examples.setup: Tangent plane to z = x² + y² at (1, 2, 5).
Estimate (3.05)² + (1.95)³ using linearisation at (3, 2).

common_mistakes: Using f_y at the wrong point.
Sign errors in (x − a) and (y − b).
Treating dz as the exact change Δz.

questions.prompt: Tangent plane formula at (a, b): z − f(a, b) = ?
Linear approx of f(x, y) = √(xy) at (1, 1).
If f_x, f_y are continuous near (a, b), then f is:

lessons.title: 14.5 The Chain Rule
lessons.explanation: CASE 1: w = f(x, y), x = x(t), y = y(t). dw/dt = f_x · dx/dt + f_y · dy/dt. CASE 2: w = f(x, y), x = x(s, t), y = y(s, t). ∂w/∂s = f_x · ∂x/∂s + f_y · ∂y/∂s; similarly for ∂w/∂t. GENERAL: build a 'tree diagram' from intermediate variables back to independent variables; multiply along each branch and sum. IMPLICIT DIFFERENTIATION: if F(x, y) = 0 defines y implicitly, then dy/dx = -F_x/F_y (provided F_y ≠ 0). For F(x, y, z) = 0: ∂z/∂x = -F_x/F_z; ∂z/∂y = -F_y/F_z.
formulas: Case 1: dw/dt = f_x x'(t) + f_y y'(t)
Case 2: ∂w/∂s = f_x ∂x/∂s + f_y ∂y/∂s
Tree diagram: multiply along branches, sum branches
Implicit (one var): dy/dx = -F_x/F_y
Implicit (3 vars): ∂z/∂x = -F_x/F_z, ∂z/∂y = -F_y/F_z

examples.setup: If z = x² + y², x = cos t, y = sin t, find dz/dt.
steps: z_x = 2x, z_y = 2y; dx/dt = -sin t, dy/dt = cos t.
dz/dt = 2x(-sin t) + 2y cos t = -2 cos t sin t + 2 sin t cos t = 0.
(Makes sense: z = 1 on the unit circle.)

examples.setup: If x² + y² + z² = 9 (sphere) implicitly defines z(x, y), find ∂z/∂x.
steps: F = x² + y² + z² − 9. F_x = 2x; F_z = 2z.

common_mistakes: Missing branches in the tree (each path back to t contributes).
Sign errors in the implicit formulas.
Treating ∂x/∂y as nonzero in case 2 (here x and y are intermediate, not independent of each other).

questions.prompt: dz/dt for z = xy, x = t, y = t².
questions.solution: z_x y'(t) +  z_y x'(t) = y · 1 + x · 2t = t² + 2t·t = 3t².

questions.prompt: Chain rule for w = f(x(t), y(t)):
dz/dt for z = ln(x), x = t².

lessons.title: 14.6 Directional Derivatives and the Gradient Vector
lessons.explanation: DIRECTIONAL DERIVATIVE: rate of change of f at (a, b) in direction of unit vector u: D_u f(a, b) = lim_{h→0} [f(a + hu_1, b + hu_2) − f(a, b)]/h = ∇f(a, b) · u, where the GRADIENT ∇f = ⟨f_x, f_y⟩ (or ⟨f_x, f_y, f_z⟩ in 3D). PROPERTIES: ∇f points in the direction of MAXIMUM increase; magnitude |∇f| is the rate of increase in that direction. The MINIMUM rate is in -∇f direction (-|∇f|). Perpendicular to ∇f: D_u f = 0 (level set tangent direction). For f(x, y, z) = c, ∇f is normal to the level surface.
formulas: Gradient: ∇f = ⟨f_x, f_y⟩ (2D) or ⟨f_x, f_y, f_z⟩ (3D)
D_u f = ∇f · u  (u must be a UNIT vector)
Maximum rate: |∇f| in direction of ∇f
Minimum rate: -|∇f| in direction of -∇f
Zero rate: u perpendicular to ∇f (along level set)
∇F is normal to level set F = c

examples.setup: Find D_u f at (1, 2) for f = x² + xy in direction of v = ⟨3, 4⟩.
Direction of fastest increase of f = x² + y² + z² at (1, 1, 1).

common_mistakes: Using non-unit direction vector (must normalise).
Treating ∇f as a scalar.
Forgetting that ∇f is perpendicular to level curves/surfaces (a key geometric fact).

questions.prompt: ∇F evaluated on level surface F = c is:

lessons.title: 14.7 Maximum and Minimum Values
lessons.explanation: f has a LOCAL MAX at (a, b) if f(a, b) ≥ f(x, y) near (a, b). At a local extremum where partials exist, f_x = f_y = 0 (a CRITICAL POINT). SECOND DERIVATIVE TEST: let D = f_xx f_yy − (f_xy)² at the critical point. (i) D > 0 and f_xx > 0: local MIN. (ii) D > 0 and f_xx < 0: local MAX. (iii) D < 0: SADDLE POINT (neither). (iv) D = 0: inconclusive. ABSOLUTE EXTREMA on a closed bounded region: find critical points inside, examine boundary (perhaps using parametrisation or constraint methods), compare values.
formulas: Critical point: f_x = f_y = 0
D = f_xx f_yy − f_xy² (Hessian determinant)
D > 0, f_xx > 0: min;  D > 0, f_xx < 0: max
D < 0: saddle
D = 0: inconclusive
Closed bounded region: critical points + boundary

examples.setup: Classify critical points of f(x, y) = x² + y² − 4x − 6y + 13.
steps: f_xx = 2, f_yy = 2, f_xy = 0; D = 4 > 0, f_xx > 0 ⇒ local MIN.

examples.setup: Classify f(x, y) = x² − y².

common_mistakes: Using sign of D alone to classify max/min — also need f_xx.
Treating D = 0 as no extremum (it's inconclusive — could be either).
Missing critical points where partials don't exist.

questions.prompt: Critical pt of f = x² + y².
f = -x² − y² has critical at (0,0). Type?

lessons.title: 14.8 Lagrange Multipliers
lessons.explanation: Optimise f(x, y) (or f(x, y, z)) subject to a CONSTRAINT g(x, y) = c. METHOD: at extrema, ∇f = λ ∇g for some scalar LAGRANGE MULTIPLIER λ. Solve the system ∇f = λ ∇g, g = c. With TWO constraints g = c, h = d: ∇f = λ ∇g + μ ∇h. GEOMETRIC IDEA: at extrema, the level curve of f is TANGENT to the constraint curve (otherwise we could move along the constraint to improve f). After finding all candidate points, compare the values of f to identify max/min.
formulas: Single constraint: ∇f = λ ∇g, g = c
Solve system: f_x = λ g_x, f_y = λ g_y, ..., g = c
Two constraints: ∇f = λ ∇g + μ ∇h
Compare values of f at all critical points

examples.setup: Maximise f = xy on x + y = 10.
steps: y = λ, x = λ ⇒ x = y. With constraint: 2x = 10 ⇒ x = 5, y = 5.

examples.setup: Minimise f = x² + y² on xy = 1.
steps: From first two: 2x · x = λxy = λ ⇒ 2x² = λ; similarly 2y² = λ.
x² = y² ⇒ x = ±y. With xy = 1, x = y = ±1.

common_mistakes: Forgetting to use the constraint to reduce the system.
Treating λ as a meaningful answer (it's a Lagrange multiplier, not the optimum).
Missing solutions where ∇g = 0 (those need separate consideration).

questions.prompt: Critical pts of f = x + y on x² + y² = 1: x = y = ?

options: Tangent of f and g level sets

questions.prompt: Max of xy on x² + y² = 2.
Min distance from origin to plane x + 2y + 3z = 14 (squared distance subject to constraint).

lessons.title: 15.1 Double Integrals over Rectangles
lessons.explanation: DOUBLE INTEGRAL ∬_R f(x, y) dA over a rectangle R = [a, b] × [c, d] is the limit of DOUBLE RIEMANN SUMS: partition R into mn small rectangles of dimensions Δx, Δy, sum f(x_i*, y_j*) ΔA. By FUBINI'S THEOREM, when f is continuous: ∬_R f dA = ∫_a^b ∫_c^d f(x, y) dy dx = ∫_c^d ∫_a^b f(x, y) dx dy. GEOMETRIC INTERPRETATION: if f ≥ 0, the integral is the volume between the surface z = f(x, y) and the rectangle R in the xy-plane. AVERAGE VALUE: f_avg = (1/Area(R)) ∬ f dA.
formulas: ∬_R f dA = lim of double Riemann sums
Fubini: ∫_a^b ∫_c^d f dy dx = ∫_c^d ∫_a^b f dx dy
Volume under z = f(x, y), f ≥ 0
Average value: f_avg = (1/Area) ∬ f dA
Linearity: ∬(c f + g) dA = c ∬f + ∬g

examples.setup: Compute ∬_R xy dA where R = [0, 1] × [0, 2].
steps: ∫₀¹ ∫₀² xy dy dx = ∫₀¹ x [y²/2]₀² dx = ∫₀¹ 2x dx = 1.
Or by Fubini: ∫₀² ∫₀¹ xy dx dy = ∫₀² y · (1/2) dy = (1/2) · 2 = 1.
∫₀² ∫₀³ (x + y) dy dx = ∫₀² [xy + y²/2]₀³ dx = ∫₀² (3x + 9/2) dx.

common_mistakes: Switching order of integration without changing limits accordingly.
Missing factors when integrating one variable at a time.
Using rectangle area as if integrand mattered separately.

options: Order of integration doesn't matter (continuous f)

questions.prompt: Average of f(x, y) = x + y over [0, 1]².

lessons.title: 15.2 Double Integrals over General Regions
lessons.explanation: Type I region: between curves y = g₁(x) and y = g₂(x) for x ∈ [a, b]. ∬_D f dA = ∫_a^b ∫_{g₁(x)}^{g₂(x)} f(x, y) dy dx. Type II region: between curves x = h₁(y) and x = h₂(y) for y ∈ [c, d]. ∬_D f dA = ∫_c^d ∫_{h₁(y)}^{h₂(y)} f(x, y) dx dy. STRATEGY: sketch the region; choose the order so that the inner limits are simpler functions. Sometimes you must change the order to make the integral tractable.
formulas: Type I: ∫_a^b ∫_{g₁(x)}^{g₂(x)} f dy dx
Type II: ∫_c^d ∫_{h₁(y)}^{h₂(y)} f dx dy
Always sketch the region first
Switch order if inner limits become simpler

examples.setup: ∬_D x dA where D is the triangle with vertices (0,0), (1,0), (1,1).
steps: ∫₀¹ ∫₀^x x dy dx = ∫₀¹ x · x dx = ∫₀¹ x² dx = 1/3.

examples.setup: ∬_D y dA where D is bounded by y = x, y = √x.
steps: Curves intersect at x = 0 and x = 1; on [0, 1], √x ≥ x.
∫₀¹ ∫_x^{√x} y dy dx = ∫₀¹ [y²/2]_x^{√x} dx = ∫₀¹ (x − x²)/2 dx.

common_mistakes: Forgetting to make the inner limits depend on the outer variable.
Mis-identifying which function is upper/lower (or right/left).
Not redrawing the region when changing order.

questions.prompt: Area of triangle vertices (0,0), (1,0), (0,1) by ∬1 dA.
∬_D x dA, D below y = x², above x-axis, on [0,1].
∬_D 1 dA where D is unit disc.
Region between y = 0, y = x, x = 1: order ∫_a^b ∫_? f dy dx, find inner upper limit.

lessons.title: 15.3 Double Integrals in Polar Coordinates
lessons.explanation: When the region or integrand has circular/radial symmetry, polar coordinates simplify. RECTANGLE in polar: R = {(r, θ) : a ≤ r ≤ b, α ≤ θ ≤ β}. AREA element: dA = r dr dθ (the extra factor r is essential — it's the JACOBIAN). ∬_R f(x, y) dA = ∫_α^β ∫_a^b f(r cos θ, r sin θ) r dr dθ. More generally, region between r = h₁(θ) and r = h₂(θ): integrate r from h₁ to h₂. Use when integrand involves x² + y² or region is a disc/sector/annulus.
formulas: Polar element: dA = r dr dθ
x = r cos θ, y = r sin θ; x² + y² = r²
Polar rectangle: ∫_α^β ∫_a^b f r dr dθ
General: ∫_α^β ∫_{h₁(θ)}^{h₂(θ)} f r dr dθ

examples.setup: Compute ∬_D (x² + y²) dA where D is the unit disc.
steps: ∫₀^(2π) ∫₀¹ r² · r dr dθ = ∫₀^(2π) [r⁴/4]₀¹ dθ = (1/4) · 2π = π/2.

examples.setup: Area of cardioid r = 1 + cos θ.
steps: A = ∫₀^(2π) ∫₀^(1+cos θ) r dr dθ = (1/2) ∫₀^(2π) (1 + cos θ)² dθ.

common_mistakes: Forgetting the extra r in dA = r dr dθ.
Wrong angular bounds (e.g. using [0, π] for full circle).
Confusing area-element with arc-length element in polar.

questions.prompt: Area of disc radius R using polar.
Area of half-disc r ≤ 3, 0 ≤ θ ≤ π.

lessons.title: 15.4 Applications of Double Integrals
lessons.explanation: MASS of a 2D plate with density ρ(x, y): m = ∬_D ρ dA. MOMENTS: M_x = ∬_D y ρ dA; M_y = ∬_D x ρ dA. CENTRE OF MASS: (x̄, ȳ) = (M_y/m, M_x/m). MOMENT OF INERTIA about an axis: e.g. I_x = ∬ y² ρ dA, I_y = ∬ x² ρ dA, I_0 = ∬ (x² + y²) ρ dA. PROBABILITY: joint PDF f(x, y) ≥ 0, ∬f dA = 1; P((X, Y) ∈ B) = ∬_B f dA. EXPECTED VALUES: μ_X = ∬ x f dA.
formulas: Mass: m = ∬ ρ dA
Moments: M_x = ∬ y ρ dA; M_y = ∬ x ρ dA
Centre of mass: (x̄, ȳ) = (M_y/m, M_x/m)
Moments of inertia: I_x = ∬ y² ρ dA; I_y = ∬ x² ρ dA; I_0 = I_x + I_y
Joint PDF f: ∬f = 1; P = ∬_B f

examples.setup: Find centre of mass of triangle vertices (0,0), (4,0), (0,2) with constant density 1.
Moment of inertia about origin for unit disc with density 1.
steps: Polar: ∫₀^(2π)∫₀¹ r² · r dr dθ = (1/4)·2π = π/2.

common_mistakes: Mixing up M_x (uses y) and M_y (uses x).
Forgetting to divide by mass when computing centre.
Treating density as a constant when it depends on (x, y).

questions.prompt: Mass of unit square with ρ = 1.
Centre of mass of unit square with ρ = 1, [0,1]×[0,1].
I_x for ρ = 1 over unit square.
Joint PDF over a region must integrate to:

lessons.title: 15.5 Surface Area
lessons.explanation: Surface area of z = f(x, y) over region D in the xy-plane: S = ∬_D √(1 + f_x² + f_y²) dA. Generalises arc length: ds → dS, with the slant factor √(1 + (∂z/∂x)² + (∂z/∂y)²). Sphere example: x² + y² + z² = R² in upper hemisphere has z = √(R² − x² − y²); computing gives surface area 4πR² for a full sphere.
formulas: Surface area: S = ∬_D √(1 + f_x² + f_y²) dA
Slant factor √(1 + f_x² + f_y²)
For x = g(y, z) over D: similar with g_y, g_z
Sphere of radius R: S = 4πR²

examples.setup: Surface area of plane z = 2x + 3y + 1 over [0, 1] × [0, 1].
Surface area of paraboloid z = x² + y² over disc r ≤ 1.

common_mistakes: Forgetting +1 inside the slant factor.
Using arc length formula instead of surface area formula.
Failing to convert to polar when natural.

questions.prompt: Slant factor for z = x² + y² at general (x, y).
Surface area of plane z = 0 over [0,2]×[0,3].
Surface area of plane z = x + y over [0,1]².
Surface element dS over xy region D:
Sphere radius R full surface area.

lessons.title: 15.6 Triple Integrals
lessons.explanation: TRIPLE INTEGRAL: ∭_E f(x, y, z) dV over solid region E. Limit of triple Riemann sums. FUBINI in 3D: integrate iteratively in any of 6 orders (when f is continuous on an appropriately regular E). Most common: ∭_E f dV = ∫∫∫ f dz dy dx with appropriate limits. VOLUME: ∭_E 1 dV = vol(E). MASS = ∭ ρ dV; CENTRE OF MASS, MOMENTS OF INERTIA generalise. STRATEGY: sketch the solid; project to one of the coordinate planes for the outer two integrals; for the inner integral, find limits of the third variable.
formulas: ∭_E f dV = lim of triple Riemann sums
Volume: V = ∭_E 1 dV
Mass: m = ∭ ρ dV
Centre of mass: x̄ = (1/m) ∭ x ρ dV (etc.)
Moment of inertia about z-axis: I_z = ∭ (x² + y²) ρ dV

examples.setup: Compute ∭_E xyz dV where E = [0,1]³.
steps: ∫₀¹∫₀¹∫₀¹ xyz dz dy dx = ∫₀¹∫₀¹ xy [z²/2]₀¹ dy dx = (1/2) ∫₀¹∫₀¹ xy dy dx.

examples.setup: Volume of tetrahedron x, y, z ≥ 0 and x + y + z ≤ 1.

common_mistakes: Wrong order of integration leading to dependent limits in the wrong variable.
Forgetting that triple integrals can have 6 orderings.
Missing inequalities defining E.

lessons.title: 15.7 Triple Integrals in Cylindrical Coordinates
lessons.explanation: Cylindrical coordinates: x = r cos θ, y = r sin θ, z = z. Volume element: dV = r dz dr dθ. Useful for solids with circular/cylindrical symmetry around the z-axis (e.g. paraboloids, cones, cylinders, washers). TYPICAL FORM: ∭_E f dV = ∫_α^β ∫_{r₁(θ)}^{r₂(θ)} ∫_{z₁(r,θ)}^{z₂(r,θ)} f(r cos θ, r sin θ, z) r dz dr dθ. Choose the outer two by projecting to the xy-plane in polar.
formulas: Cylindrical: x = r cos θ, y = r sin θ, z = z
Volume element: dV = r dz dr dθ
Best for solids with symmetry around the z-axis
x² + y² = r² in cylindrical

Choosing wrong order when z-limits depend on r and θ.

lessons.title: 15.8 Triple Integrals in Spherical Coordinates
lessons.explanation: Spherical coordinates: x = ρ sin φ cos θ, y = ρ sin φ sin θ, z = ρ cos φ, where ρ ≥ 0, 0 ≤ φ ≤ π (polar angle from positive z), 0 ≤ θ < 2π (azimuth). Volume element: dV = ρ² sin φ dρ dφ dθ. Best for solids defined by ρ-bounds: balls, spherical shells, cones. The Jacobian ρ² sin φ is essential — never omit it.
formulas: Spherical: x = ρ sin φ cos θ, y = ρ sin φ sin θ, z = ρ cos φ
ρ² = x² + y² + z²
Volume element: dV = ρ² sin φ dρ dφ dθ
Best for: balls, spherical shells, cones from origin

common_mistakes: Omitting ρ² sin φ in the Jacobian.
Confusing φ (polar angle from z-axis) with θ (azimuth).
Using bounds beyond their natural range.

lessons.title: 15.9 Change of Variables in Multiple Integrals
lessons.explanation: For a substitution u = u(x, y), v = v(x, y) with Jacobian J = ∂(x, y)/∂(u, v) = det[x_u, x_v; y_u, y_v]: ∬_R f(x, y) dA = ∬_S f(x(u,v), y(u,v)) |J| du dv. POLAR is a special case (J = r). Choose substitution to: (1) simplify the integrand, or (2) simplify the region (turn it into a rectangle in uv). EXAMPLE: u = x + y, v = x − y has Jacobian 1/2 (or |J| = 1/2). Triple version analogous with 3×3 Jacobian.
formulas: 2D: ∬ f dA = ∬ f(x(u,v), y(u,v)) |∂(x,y)/∂(u,v)| du dv
Jacobian: J = det[x_u x_v; y_u y_v]
Polar: J = r
Cylindrical: J = r;  Spherical: J = ρ² sin φ
3D: |∂(x,y,z)/∂(u,v,w)| = 3×3 determinant

topics.title: Vector Fields, Line/Surface Integrals, and the Big Theorems

lessons.title: 16.1 Vector Fields
lessons.explanation: A VECTOR FIELD on a region D ⊂ ℝ² (or ℝ³) is a map F: D → ℝ² (or ℝ³) assigning a vector to each point. In 2D: F(x, y) = ⟨P(x, y), Q(x, y)⟩. In 3D: F(x, y, z) = ⟨P, Q, R⟩. Visualised by drawing arrows of length |F| at each point. Examples: gravitational field (vectors pointing toward a mass), velocity field of a fluid, electric field, magnetic field. A GRADIENT field is F = ∇f for some scalar function f (called a POTENTIAL). Such fields are called CONSERVATIVE — a foundational concept in physics (energy conservation).
formulas: 2D: F(x, y) = ⟨P(x, y), Q(x, y)⟩
3D: F(x, y, z) = ⟨P, Q, R⟩
Gradient field: F = ∇f = ⟨f_x, f_y, f_z⟩
Conservative ⇔ F = ∇f for some f (the potential)
Examples: gravity, fluid velocity, electromagnetic fields

examples.setup: Sketch F(x, y) = ⟨-y, x⟩.
steps: Pattern: anticlockwise rotation field around origin.

examples.setup: Find ∇f for f(x, y, z) = x² + y² + z².

common_mistakes: Treating a vector field as a scalar function.
Plotting only at a few points and missing the global pattern.
Assuming every vector field is conservative (most aren't).

lessons.title: 16.2 Line Integrals
lessons.explanation: Two flavours, with same idea: integrate along a curve C. (i) SCALAR line integral: ∫_C f ds = ∫_a^b f(r(t)) |r'(t)| dt — sums values of f along C weighted by arc length. Used for total mass of a wire with linear density f. (ii) VECTOR line integral (work done by F): ∫_C F · dr = ∫_a^b F(r(t)) · r'(t) dt. Sums tangential components of F. Sign depends on direction of traversal. ∫_C P dx + Q dy + R dz uses the parameterisation to convert each differential.
formulas: Scalar: ∫_C f ds = ∫_a^b f(r(t)) |r'(t)| dt
Vector (work): ∫_C F · dr = ∫_a^b F(r(t)) · r'(t) dt
Differential form: ∫_C P dx + Q dy + R dz
Direction: ∫_(-C) F · dr = -∫_C F · dr
Used for: mass of wire, work, circulation

examples.setup: Compute ∫_C x ds where C: r(t) = ⟨t, t²⟩, t ∈ [0, 1].
Work of F = ⟨y, x⟩ along r(t) = ⟨cos t, sin t⟩, 0 ≤ t ≤ π/2.
steps: F(r(t)) = ⟨sin t, cos t⟩; r'(t) = ⟨-sin t, cos t⟩.

common_mistakes: Using ds when the problem wants F · dr (or vice versa).
Forgetting the |r'(t)| factor in scalar line integrals.
Not negating when reversing direction.

lessons.title: 16.3 The Fundamental Theorem for Line Integrals
lessons.explanation: If F = ∇f (conservative field, with f the potential), then ∫_C F · dr = f(B) − f(A), where A, B are the endpoints of C. Consequence: PATH-INDEPENDENT — only endpoints matter. Closed curves give 0. TEST FOR CONSERVATIVE in 2D: F = ⟨P, Q⟩ on a simply connected region is conservative iff ∂P/∂y = ∂Q/∂x. In 3D: ∇ × F = 0 (curl zero) on simply connected region. FINDING f: integrate P w.r.t. x, take ∂/∂y, match to Q to find missing y-only piece.
formulas: FTLI: F = ∇f ⇒ ∫_C F · dr = f(B) − f(A)
Closed curve: ∮_C F · dr = 0 if F conservative
2D test: P_y = Q_x on simply connected region ⇔ conservative
3D test: ∇ × F = 0 on simply connected region ⇔ conservative
Find f: f = ∫P dx + h(y); use Q to determine h

examples.setup: Show F = ⟨2xy, x²⟩ is conservative and find a potential.
steps: f_y = x² + h'(y) must equal Q = x², so h'(y) = 0, h = const.

examples.setup: Compute ∫_C F · dr from (0, 0) to (1, 2) where F = ⟨2xy, x²⟩.
steps: F is conservative with f = x²y.

common_mistakes: Applying FTLI to non-conservative fields.
Skipping the simply connected hypothesis.
Adding +C inappropriately when finding potentials (any constant works).

lessons.title: 16.4 Green's Theorem
lessons.explanation: Connects a LINE integral around a closed curve to a DOUBLE integral over the region it encloses. For a positively oriented (anticlockwise) simple closed curve C bounding region D: ∮_C P dx + Q dy = ∬_D (∂Q/∂x − ∂P/∂y) dA. AREA via line integral: A = ∮_C x dy = -∮_C y dx = (1/2) ∮_C (x dy − y dx). If F is conservative (P_y = Q_x), the right side is 0, recovering FTLI for closed curves. Generalisation of FTC to 2D.
formulas: Green's: ∮_C P dx + Q dy = ∬_D (Q_x − P_y) dA
Anticlockwise orientation
Area: A = (1/2) ∮_C (x dy − y dx)
Special case: F conservative ⇒ both sides zero

examples.setup: Use Green's: ∮_C (-y) dx + x dy where C is unit circle anticlockwise.
Compute area of unit disc via line integral.
steps: Parameterise C: x = cos t, y = sin t, t ∈ [0, 2π].
A = (1/2) ∮ (x dy − y dx) = (1/2) ∫₀^(2π) (cos t · cos t − sin t · (-sin t)) dt.

common_mistakes: Using Green's with clockwise orientation without flipping sign.
Forgetting positive orientation requirement.
Mis-computing Q_x − P_y (sign of P_y).

questions.prompt: Area of region using Green's: A = (1/2) ∮_C (?).
∮_C 0 dx + x dy on unit circle (Green's).
questions.solution: Q_x − P_y = 1 − 0 = 1; ∬ 1 dA over disc = π.

lessons.title: 16.5 Curl and Divergence
lessons.explanation: CURL of F = ⟨P, Q, R⟩: ∇ × F = ⟨R_y − Q_z, P_z − R_x, Q_x − P_y⟩. Measures infinitesimal rotation. For 2D F = ⟨P, Q⟩, scalar curl = Q_x − P_y. DIVERGENCE: ∇ · F = P_x + Q_y + R_z. Measures the rate at which F spreads out from a point. PROPERTIES: ∇ × ∇f = 0 (curl of gradient is zero); ∇ · (∇ × F) = 0 (divergence of curl is zero). F conservative ⇒ ∇ × F = 0 (and converse on simply connected). F is INCOMPRESSIBLE if ∇ · F = 0; IRROTATIONAL if ∇ × F = 0.
formulas: Curl: ∇ × F = ⟨R_y − Q_z, P_z − R_x, Q_x − P_y⟩
Div: ∇ · F = P_x + Q_y + R_z
∇ × (∇f) = 0 (always)
∇ · (∇ × F) = 0 (always)
Laplacian: ∇²f = ∇ · (∇f) = f_xx + f_yy + f_zz

examples.setup: Find ∇ · F and ∇ × F for F = ⟨xy, yz, zx⟩.
Show ∇ × (∇f) = 0 for f = x² + y² + z².
steps: Curl: R_y − Q_z = 0; P_z − R_x = 0; Q_x − P_y = 0.

common_mistakes: Mixing up curl and div (curl is vector, div is scalar).
Sign errors in the curl components.
Forgetting that simply connected is required for curl = 0 ⇒ conservative.

questions.prompt: Laplacian of f = x² + y² + z².

lessons.title: 16.6 Parametric Surfaces and Their Areas
lessons.explanation: A PARAMETRIC SURFACE: r(u, v) = ⟨x(u, v), y(u, v), z(u, v)⟩ for (u, v) in some domain D. Examples: sphere r(φ, θ) = ⟨R sin φ cos θ, R sin φ sin θ, R cos φ⟩; cylinder r(θ, z) = ⟨R cos θ, R sin θ, z⟩. TANGENT VECTORS: r_u = ∂r/∂u, r_v = ∂r/∂v. The cross product r_u × r_v is normal to the surface. SURFACE AREA: S = ∬_D |r_u × r_v| dA. For surface z = f(x, y) with natural parametrisation r(x, y) = ⟨x, y, f(x, y)⟩: |r_x × r_y| = √(1 + f_x² + f_y²).
formulas: Parametric surface: r(u, v) = ⟨x(u, v), y(u, v), z(u, v)⟩
Tangent vectors: r_u, r_v
Normal vector: r_u × r_v
Surface area: S = ∬_D |r_u × r_v| dA
For z = f(x, y): |r_x × r_y| = √(1 + f_x² + f_y²)

examples.setup: Surface area of sphere of radius R using r(φ, θ) = ⟨R sin φ cos θ, R sin φ sin θ, R cos φ⟩.
steps: r_φ = ⟨R cos φ cos θ, R cos φ sin θ, -R sin φ⟩.
r_θ = ⟨-R sin φ sin θ, R sin φ cos θ, 0⟩.
S = ∫₀^(2π) ∫₀^π R² sin φ dφ dθ = R² · 2 · 2π = 4πR².

examples.setup: Surface area of cylinder x² + y² = R², 0 ≤ z ≤ h.
steps: r(θ, z) = ⟨R cos θ, R sin θ, z⟩.
S = ∫₀^(2π) ∫₀^h R dz dθ = R · h · 2π = 2πRh.

common_mistakes: Forgetting to take magnitude of r_u × r_v.
Confusing parametric surface with parametric curve.
Picking parametrisation that doesn't cover the surface exactly once.

lessons.title: 16.7 Surface Integrals
lessons.explanation: SCALAR surface integral: ∬_S f dS = ∬_D f(r(u, v)) |r_u × r_v| dA. VECTOR surface integral (FLUX): ∬_S F · dS = ∬_S F · n dS = ∬_D F(r(u, v)) · (r_u × r_v) dA. Measures rate of flow of F through S. ORIENTATION matters — flipping the normal flips the sign. For closed surfaces, OUTWARD normal is conventional. APPLICATIONS: fluid flow, electric flux (Gauss's law), heat flow.
formulas: Scalar: ∬_S f dS = ∬_D f(r) |r_u × r_v| dA
Vector (flux): ∬_S F · dS = ∬_D F · (r_u × r_v) dA
Orientation flips sign
Closed surfaces: outward normal convention
For z = g(x, y): F · dS = (-P g_x − Q g_y + R) dA

lessons.title: 16.8 Stokes' Theorem
lessons.explanation: STOKES' THEOREM relates a LINE integral around a closed curve to a SURFACE integral of the curl over any surface bounded by the curve: ∮_C F · dr = ∬_S (∇ × F) · dS. Generalises Green's Theorem to 3D. C must be POSITIVELY ORIENTED relative to the chosen normal on S (right-hand rule: thumb along normal, fingers curl in direction of C). STRATEGY: choose the simplest surface bounded by C (often a flat disc or a paraboloid), compute curl, integrate.
formulas: Stokes': ∮_C F · dr = ∬_S (∇ × F) · dS
Right-hand rule: thumb along normal, fingers curl with C
Choose simplest bounding surface for S
Reduces to Green's Theorem in xy-plane

lessons.title: 16.9 The Divergence Theorem
lessons.explanation: Also called GAUSS'S THEOREM. Relates the FLUX of F through a closed surface S (outward normal) to the integral of div F over the enclosed solid E: ∬_S F · dS = ∭_E (∇ · F) dV. Generalises Green's flux form to 3D and parallels FTC and Stokes' as 'fundamental theorems'. APPLICATIONS: physical flux laws (Gauss's law for electricity: total flux of electric field through closed surface = enclosed charge / ε₀); fluid mechanics; heat conduction. Often dramatically simplifies flux through complicated closed surfaces.
formulas: Divergence Theorem: ∬_S F · dS = ∭_E (∇ · F) dV
S is closed; outward normal
E is the solid enclosed by S
Gauss's law (electrostatics): ∬_S E · dS = Q_enclosed / ε₀

Verify Divergence Theorem for F = ⟨x, 0, 0⟩ over unit cube [0,1]³.
steps: Direct flux: only x = 0 and x = 1 faces contribute.
x = 1 face: F · n = 1 · 1 = 1, area 1, flux 1.

common_mistakes: Using the theorem on non-closed surfaces (it doesn't apply).
Inward instead of outward normal.
Forgetting the solid E must be bounded by the surface S.

lessons.title: 16.10 Summary: Unifying the Fundamental Theorems
lessons.explanation: The big theorems all follow the pattern: the integral of a derivative over a region equals the integral of the original over the boundary. FTC: ∫_a^b F'(x) dx = F(b) − F(a) — interval to its endpoints. FTLI: ∫_C ∇f · dr = f(B) − f(A) — curve to its endpoints. GREEN'S: ∬_D (Q_x − P_y) dA = ∮_C P dx + Q dy — region to its boundary curve. STOKES': ∬_S (∇ × F) · dS = ∮_C F · dr — surface to its boundary curve. DIVERGENCE: ∭_E (∇ · F) dV = ∬_S F · dS — solid to its boundary surface. All are special cases of the GENERALISED STOKES' THEOREM ∫_M dω = ∫_∂M ω from differential geometry — a single formula unifying everything.
formulas: FTC: ∫ F' = F(boundary)
FTLI: ∫_C ∇f · dr = f(boundary)
Green's: ∬_D (Q_x − P_y) dA = ∮_∂D
Stokes': ∬_S curl F · dS = ∮_∂S
Divergence: ∭_E div F dV = ∬_∂E
All: ∫ (something) = ∫_boundary (one less dimension)

Same answer — Green's is Stokes' in the plane.

examples.setup: Use Divergence to bypass parametrising a complicated closed surface.
steps: If F has simple divergence (like ∇ · F = 0 — incompressible), flux through ANY closed surface is 0.
Example: F = ⟨y, -x, 0⟩ has ∇ · F = 0; flux through any closed surface = 0.
examples.result: Divergence theorem simplifies dramatically when div F is simple.

common_mistakes: Forgetting the orientation conventions (anticlockwise / outward).
Applying a theorem to the wrong dimensional pairing.
Trying to compute everything by direct parametrisation when a theorem yields immediate answers.
